course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Which of the following best describes the social dimension of adulthood?
option_a: Reaching the legal age at which privileges are granted and duties are required
option_b: Being perceived as a mature, rational, responsible, and socially dependable person
option_c: Attaining full physical size, strength, and reproductive capacity
option_d: Developing emotional stability and breaking childish dependency ties with parents
rationale: The social dimension of adulthood reflects a mature, rational, and responsible person. It is the primary meaning of adulthood, as one cannot simply declare oneself an adult — the perception must come from society. An undisciplined, irrational, or socially irresponsible person would not be considered an adult regardless of their biological or legal status.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to the course material, how many distinct dimensions of adulthood are identified?
option_a: Two — biological and social
option_b: Three — physical, cognitive, and emotional
option_c: Four — social, biological, emotional, and legal
option_d: Five — social, biological, emotional, legal, and spiritual
rationale: The course material identifies four dimensions of adulthood: social (mature, rational, responsible person), biological (full size, strength, reproductive capacity), emotional (emotional stability, impulse control, autonomy), and legal (chronological ages at which privileges and duties are assigned).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why is the biological dimension alone considered inadequate for defining adulthood?
option_a: Because biological maturity occurs at different ages across all cultures
option_b: Because adolescents may reach full height and sexual maturity while still behaving childishly
option_c: Because the biological dimension does not account for chronological age
option_d: Because biological maturity typically occurs after legal adulthood is reached
rationale: The biological definition of adulthood is inadequate because adolescents in many cultures may attain their full height, strength, and sexual maturity but still behave in childish, dependent ways with nebulous identities and undetermined stations in life. Physical maturity alone does not guarantee adult behaviour or social readiness.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A young adult has reached full physical development, can legally vote and marry without parental consent, and demonstrates good impulse control. However, they frequently shirk work responsibilities and are considered unreliable by peers and employers. Based on the four dimensions of adulthood, which conclusion is most accurate?
option_a: The individual has fully achieved adulthood across all four dimensions
option_b: The individual has achieved adulthood in the biological, legal, and emotional dimensions but not in the social dimension
option_c: The individual is deficient only in the emotional dimension of adulthood
option_d: The individual has achieved adulthood only in the biological and legal dimensions
rationale: The social dimension of adulthood requires the person to be perceived as mature, rational, responsible, and dependable. Since the individual shirks work responsibilities and is considered unreliable, they have not achieved the social dimension. They demonstrate biological maturity (full physical development), legal adulthood (can vote and marry), and emotional maturity (good impulse control).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material describes adulthood as lacking the "concrete boundaries" that define infancy, childhood, and adolescence. Which statement best explains why adulthood has been referred to as a "catch-all category" in scientific literature?
option_a: Because adulthood is legally defined at different ages across different countries
option_b: Because theorists like Freud, Piaget, and Kohlberg viewed adult development as minimal or as a plateau after earlier stages
option_c: Because the physical changes during adulthood are less predictable than those in childhood
option_d: Because adulthood is subdivided into fewer periods than other life stages
rationale: The course material notes that Sigmund Freud viewed adult life as merely a ripple on the surface of an already set personality structure; Jean Piaget assumed no additional cognitive changes occur after adolescence; and Lawrence Kohlberg saw moral development as reaching a lifetime plateau after early adulthood. These perspectives contributed to adulthood functioning as a catch-all category for everything that happens after an individual grows up.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 19-year-old has reached their full adult height, is sexually mature, and has a legal driver's licence. However, they still rely heavily on their parents for financial decisions, throw tantrums when frustrated, and are described by their community as irresponsible. Which dimension of adulthood is this individual primarily lacking in?
option_a: Biological and legal dimensions only
option_b: Social and emotional dimensions
option_c: Legal dimension only
option_d: All four dimensions
rationale: The individual has achieved the biological dimension (full height, sexual maturity) and the legal dimension (driver's licence). However, their reliance on parents for decisions and tantrums indicate a lack of emotional maturity (emotional dimension), while being called irresponsible indicates a lack of social maturity (social dimension). The social dimension reflects a mature, rational, and socially responsible person, while the emotional dimension implies emotional stability and breaking childish dependency ties.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: During a family therapy session, a therapist notes that a 22-year-old client has achieved full physical growth, is emotionally stable with good frustration tolerance, and has reached the age of legal majority. However, the client's parents describe them as undependable and socially irresponsible. The therapist concludes that the client has not yet achieved full adulthood. Which reasoning best supports this conclusion?
option_a: The client has not yet reached the biological milestones of adulthood
option_b: The client's legal status is not recognised in the jurisdiction
option_c: The client lacks the social dimension, which is the primary meaning of adulthood
option_d: The client cannot be considered an adult without completing higher education
rationale: The social dimension is the primary meaning of adulthood — one cannot declare oneself an adult; this perception reflects a mature, rational, and responsible person. It is unthinkable to call an undisciplined, irrational, undependable, socially irresponsible person an adult. Despite meeting biological, emotional, and legal criteria, the client's lack of social maturity means they have not fully achieved adulthood.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A high school counsellor is advising students about to turn 18 on what adulthood actually entails. Which of the following statements would be the most educationally accurate to include in the guidance session?
option_a: "Once you turn 18, you are biologically, socially, emotionally, and legally an adult in all respects."
option_b: "Legal adulthood at 18 gives you rights, but social and emotional maturity may take longer to develop."
option_c: "Adulthood is determined primarily by biological changes that begin at age 18."
option_d: "Emotional maturity is the only dimension that matters for becoming an adult."
rationale: Adulthood means different things for different people and has multiple dimensions. While the legal dimension grants privileges and duties at age 18, biological, social, and emotional maturity may not align with this chronological marker. Adolescents may reach full size and sexual maturity but still behave childishly. The legal dimension designates chronological ages at which privileges are granted, but this does not automatically guarantee maturity across all dimensions.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher studying adult development interviews a 30-year-old who has completed university, lives independently, holds a steady job, and demonstrates strong emotional regulation. However, the interviewee still allows their parents to make major life decisions for them. When categorising this individual's developmental status, the researcher should note that:
option_a: The individual has fully met all criteria for adulthood across the four dimensions
option_b: The individual has achieved biological, legal, and emotional adulthood but shows incomplete social adulthood due to persistent dependency
option_c: The individual has only achieved the legal dimension of adulthood
option_d: The individual cannot be classified as an adult until they achieve financial independence
rationale: The emotional dimension of adulthood includes breaking childish dependency ties with parents and functioning autonomously. Allowing parents to make major life decisions indicates persistent dependency ties, which is a failure of emotional maturity. However, the individual demonstrates biological maturity (30 years old), legal adulthood, and emotional stability (strong regulation), suggesting partial achievement across dimensions.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: In a cross-cultural study of adulthood, researchers find that in one culture, individuals are considered adults once they complete puberty, while in another, the legal age of majority is 21. Despite these differences, both cultures expect adults to demonstrate rational decision-making and responsibility toward their community. Which conclusion does this finding best support?
option_a: The biological dimension is the most important across all cultures
option_b: The social dimension of adulthood has cross-cultural validity as a core marker
option_c: Legal definitions of adulthood are universally consistent
option_d: Emotional maturity is not relevant in collectivist cultures
rationale: The social dimension reflects a mature, rational, and socially responsible person — this is described as the primary meaning of adulthood. While biological and legal markers vary across cultures, the expectation of rational, responsible behaviour (social dimension) appears consistent, suggesting this dimension has cross-cultural validity as a core marker of adult status.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A developmental psychologist is assessing whether a 25-year-old foster care youth has successfully transitioned to independent adulthood. Which set of criteria would provide the most comprehensive assessment based on the four dimensions?
option_a: Checking if the individual has a high school diploma and stable employment
option_b: Evaluating physical maturity, legal age status, emotional stability, and social responsibility
option_c: Measuring only the individual's emotional intelligence quotient
option_d: Assessing whether the individual has established their own family unit
rationale: Adulthood is multidimensional, encompassing social (mature, rational, responsible person), biological (full size, strength, reproductive capacity), emotional (emotional stability, autonomy), and legal (age-related privileges and duties) dimensions. A comprehensive assessment must evaluate all four dimensions rather than relying on any single indicator such as education, employment, emotional intelligence, or family formation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A policy maker is designing a programme to help young people transition from adolescence to adulthood. Based on the four dimensions of adulthood, which component should be prioritised to ensure holistic development?
option_a: A focus exclusively on vocational training to ensure economic independence
option_b: Programmes that develop social responsibility, emotional regulation, and autonomy alongside practical skills
option_c: Legal education about rights and responsibilities at age 18
option_d: Physical health and fitness programmes only
rationale: Since adulthood comprises four distinct dimensions — social, biological, emotional, and legal — a holistic transition programme must address all of them. Vocational training alone addresses only economic aspects; legal education only addresses the legal dimension; and fitness programmes only address biological aspects. Programmes that develop social responsibility, emotional regulation, and autonomy alongside practical skills would comprehensively address the multidimensional nature of adulthood.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 17-year-old, who has not yet reached the legal age of adulthood, demonstrates remarkable maturity by volunteering in their community, managing their emotions effectively during crises, and being physically fully grown. Their community members frequently describe them as "more adult than many adults." This scenario best illustrates which concept about adulthood?
option_a: Legal adulthood is the only valid measure of adult status
option_b: The dimensions of adulthood can be achieved independently and do not always align with chronological or legal markers
option_c: Biological maturity always precedes social and emotional maturity
option_d: Adolescents cannot demonstrate any dimension of adulthood before age 18
rationale: Adulthood means different things for different people. This individual demonstrates social maturity (community volunteering), emotional maturity (managing emotions effectively), and biological maturity (physically fully grown) despite not having reached the legal age of adulthood. This illustrates that the dimensions of adulthood can be achieved independently and do not necessarily align with chronological or legal markers.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A developmental researcher compares two individuals: Person A is 16 years old, has reached full physical maturity, demonstrates excellent emotional regulation, and is described by teachers as highly responsible. Person B is 25 years old, has a legal drinking age status, but behaves childishly, depends financially on parents, and is considered unreliable by peers. Which analysis best applies the concept of adulthood dimensions to these cases?
option_a: Person A is legally an adult, while Person B is not
option_b: Person A has achieved more dimensions of adulthood than Person B despite being younger
option_c: Person B is an adult because chronological age is the primary determinant
option_d: Both individuals have achieved adulthood in the same number of dimensions
rationale: Person A demonstrates biological maturity (full physical maturity), emotional maturity (excellent regulation), and social maturity (described as highly responsible) — three dimensions — despite being below legal adult age. Person B demonstrates only legal adulthood (drinking age status) but lacks emotional maturity (behaves childishly, depends on parents) and social maturity (unreliable). This comparison shows that more dimensions of adulthood can be achieved at a younger age when the individual demonstrates maturity across multiple domains.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Three developmental theorists — Freud, Piaget, and Kohlberg — are discussed in the course material as having contributed to the view of adulthood as a "catch-all category." What common limitation in their theories led to this categorisation?
option_a: They all believed adulthood was the most important stage of human development
option_b: They each assumed that significant development largely ceases after adolescence or early adulthood
option_c: They focused exclusively on biological changes during adulthood
option_d: They rejected the idea that adulthood has multiple dimensions
rationale: Freud viewed adult life as merely a ripple on the surface of an already set personality structure; Piaget assumed that no additional cognitive changes occur after adolescence; and Kohlberg saw moral development as reaching a lifetime plateau after early adulthood. The common thread is that each theorist assumed significant development largely ceases after earlier stages, leaving adulthood as a residual category for everything that happens after growing up.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher proposes that the legal age of adulthood should be raised from 18 to 25, arguing that most 18-year-olds have not yet achieved biological maturity, emotional stability, or social responsibility. Which critique of this proposal is most consistent with the course material?
option_a: The proposal correctly identifies that no 18-year-olds are biologically mature
option_b: The proposal ignores that adulthood dimensions are achieved independently; some 18-year-olds demonstrate social and emotional maturity while some 25-year-olds do not
option_c: The proposal should focus exclusively on the emotional dimension
option_d: The proposal is consistent with the view that adulthood has concrete boundaries
rationale: The course material explicitly states that the category of adulthood lacks the concrete boundaries of infancy, childhood, and adolescence, and that adulthood means different things for different people. Adolescents may reach full size and sexual maturity but still behave childishly. Conversely, some younger individuals demonstrate remarkable social and emotional maturity. The dimensions of adulthood are achieved independently and do not uniformly align with any single chronological age.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: In an educational psychology study, two groups of 18-year-olds are assessed. Group X scored high on measures of emotional stability and social responsibility but had below-average physical fitness. Group Y scored high on physical fitness and athletic ability but low on emotional regulation and social dependability. Which interpretation best aligns with the multidimensional view of adulthood?
option_a: Group X has achieved more dimensions of adulthood than Group Y
option_b: Group Y is more adult because physical fitness is the most important dimension
option_c: Neither group can be considered adult until all dimensions are fully achieved
option_d: Both groups are equally adult because they each excel in one dimension
rationale: Group X demonstrates emotional maturity (emotional stability) and social maturity (social responsibility) — two key dimensions. Group Y demonstrates biological maturity (physical fitness, athletic ability) but lacks emotional maturity (poor regulation) and social maturity (low dependability). The social dimension is described as the primary meaning of adulthood, and emotional maturity includes emotional stability. Therefore, Group X has achieved more of the defining dimensions of adulthood.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A sociologist argues that in modern society, the legal dimension of adulthood has become more important than the social dimension because legal rights (voting, marriage, contracts) are what actually determine adult status. Which response to this argument best reflects the course material's perspective?
option_a: The argument is valid because legal rights are the only objective measure of adulthood
option_b: The argument contradicts the material, which states that the social dimension is the primary meaning of adulthood
option_c: The argument is supported because legal adulthood was recently lowered to 18
option_d: The argument is irrelevant because legal dimensions do not exist in most cultures
rationale: The course material explicitly states that the social dimension is the primary meaning of adulthood and that one cannot declare oneself an adult — this perception reflects a mature, rational, responsible, and dependable person. While the legal dimension designates chronological ages at which privileges are granted, it is not presented as more important than the social dimension. The social dimension involves being perceived by society as mature and responsible.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Some policymakers advocate for a single, standardised test of emotional and social maturity to determine when an individual qualifies for full adult rights and responsibilities. Based on the course material's treatment of the dimensions of adulthood, which is the strongest evaluation of this proposal?
option_a: It is an excellent approach that would resolve all ambiguity around adult status
option_b: It is fundamentally flawed because adulthood is multidimensional and lacks concrete boundaries; no single test could capture all four dimensions
option_c: It should be adopted but only for the biological dimension of adulthood
option_d: It is unnecessary because all individuals achieve all dimensions of adulthood at age 18
rationale: The course material emphasises that adulthood lacks the concrete boundaries of earlier developmental stages and means different things for different people. The four dimensions — social, biological, emotional, and legal — are distinct and achieved independently. The social dimension is the primary meaning and involves societal perception. A single standardised test cannot capture the full complexity of multidimensional adulthood, and the material explicitly rejects fixed boundaries for this stage.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A youth development programme claims that it can guarantee full adult maturity in its graduates by age 18 through intensive training in emotional regulation, social skills, and vocational competence. Which critique of this claim is most supported by the course material?
option_a: The claim is valid because intensive training can ensure all dimensions are achieved simultaneously
option_b: The claim overpromises because the dimensions of adulthood are achieved independently and at different rates for different individuals
option_c: The claim should focus exclusively on biological maturity instead
option_d: The claim is irrelevant because adulthood is determined solely by legal age
rationale: The course material states that adulthood means different things for different people and lacks concrete boundaries. The four dimensions — social, biological, emotional, and legal — are achieved independently. Furthermore, the material notes that adolescents may reach full size and sexual maturity but still behave childishly. This individual variation means no programme can guarantee simultaneous achievement of all dimensions by a fixed age.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to Kastenbaum, which type of age refers to how old a person's body seems to be physically?
option_a: Chronological age
option_b: Subjective age
option_c: Biological age
option_d: Functional age
rationale: Kastenbaum (1979) identified five types of age that individuals coexist with. Biological age refers to how old the body seems to be physically — the sort of shape a person is in physically. It is distinct from chronological age (how many years lived), subjective age (how old one feels), functional age (age associated with job or role), and social age (the age group one mixes with).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Kastenbaum proposed that individuals coexist with how many different "ages" simultaneously?
option_a: Three
option_b: Four
option_c: Five
option_d: Six
rationale: Kastenbaum (1979) suggested that individuals coexist with five different "ages": chronological age (how many years lived), biological age (how old the body seems physically), subjective age (how old one actually feels), functional age (age associated with one's job or role), and social age (the age group one mixes with and how others see one's personal style).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 65-year-old retired accountant continues to work part-time as a financial consultant, feels mentally sharp and energetic, and spends time with friends who are in their 50s. His friends describe him as "young at heart." According to Kastenbaum's framework, which type of age is most reflected by his friends' description?
option_a: Chronological age
option_b: Functional age
option_c: Subjective age
option_d: Social age
rationale: Subjective age refers to how old a person actually feels themselves to be. The description "young at heart" captures the individual's internal, subjective experience of their own age rather than their chronological years (65), their functional role (consultant), or the age of their social circle (50s). Kastenbaum's framework validates that people often report feeling or acting older or younger than their chronological years.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why does the course material suggest that Kastenbaum's concept of multiple ages has "face validity"?
option_a: Because it has been statistically validated across all cultures
option_b: Because many people report acting or feeling older or younger than their years
option_c: Because it aligns with the legal definition of age
option_d: Because it was endorsed by all major developmental theorists
rationale: The course material states that Kastenbaum's concept certainly has face validity as so many people report acting or feeling older or younger than their years, depending on what they are doing. The example of Mick Jagger being featured on a magazine for retired people supports the view that adulthood and older adulthood are concepts interpreted differently by different groups of people.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 45-year-old individual takes on the role of CEO for the first time, overseeing a large corporation. They note that colleagues treat them differently now compared to when they were a junior manager. Which type of age best explains this change in how they are perceived?
option_a: Chronological age, because they are now 45
option_b: Functional age, because their responsibilities and role have changed
option_c: Biological age, because the role has aged them physically
option_d: Social age, because they now socialise with different people
rationale: Functional age is the age associated with a person's job, role, and responsibilities. When the individual moved from junior manager to CEO, their functional age changed because the role and its associated responsibilities changed dramatically. The difference in how colleagues treat them reflects this shift in functional age rather than their chronological, biological, or social age.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 70-year-old marathon runner has the cardiovascular fitness of a typical 50-year-old, trains with a running club whose members are mostly in their 40s and 50s, and describes herself as "feeling about 45." When assessing her overall aging profile using Kastenbaum's framework, which combination of ages applies?
option_a: Chronological: 70, Biological: 50, Functional: 70, Social: 70, Subjective: 45
option_b: Chronological: 70, Biological: 50, Functional: 70, Social: 40-50s, Subjective: 45
option_c: Chronological: 70, Biological: 70, Functional: 50, Social: 40-50s, Subjective: 70
option_d: Chronological: 50, Biological: 50, Functional: 70, Social: 40-50s, Subjective: 45
rationale: Using Kastenbaum's framework: Chronological age = 70 years lived. Biological age = approximately 50 (cardiovascular fitness of a 50-year-old). Functional age = 70 (associated with her role in society). Social age = 40-50s (the age group she mixes with in her running club). Subjective age = 45 (how old she feels). This illustrates how the five ages can diverge significantly within one individual.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 55-year-old university professor is told by his doctor that despite his chronological age, his biological markers (blood pressure, cholesterol, joint health) are equivalent to those of a typical 70-year-old due to years of sedentary work and poor diet. However, he feels mentally sharp and his students describe him as energetic. According to Kastenbaum's framework, which analysis is most accurate?
option_a: All five of his ages are aligned at approximately 55
option_b: His chronological age is 55, biological age is approximately 70, but his functional and subjective ages may differ
option_c: His chronological age is 70 because that is his biological age
option_d: His functional age is 70 because of his sedentary work
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework acknowledges that individuals coexist with several ages rather than a single numerical age. The professor's chronological age is 55 (years lived), biological age is approximately 70 (physical condition), but his functional age relates to his role as a professor and his subjective age reflects how he feels (mentally sharp, energetic). This demonstrates that different types of age can diverge significantly within one person.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A gerontologist is designing an intervention programme for older adults. She decides to include components that address each of Kastenbaum's five ages. Which programme component best addresses social age?
option_a: Exercise classes to improve cardiovascular health
option_b: Group activities that bring participants together with peers of similar interests
option_c: Cognitive training sessions to maintain mental sharpness
option_d: Vocational retraining workshops for new roles
rationale: Social age relates to the age group one mixes with and how others see one's personal style. Group activities that bring participants together with peers of similar interests directly address the social dimension of aging by fostering age-appropriate social connections. Exercise classes address biological age, cognitive training addresses subjective aspects of functioning, and vocational workshops address functional age.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 60-year-old woman who recently retired reports feeling "invisible" because her younger colleagues no longer seek her input on projects. She notes that her sense of identity was strongly tied to her professional role. According to Kastenbaum's framework, which type of age is she most likely experiencing difficulty with?
option_a: Chronological age
option_b: Biological age
option_c: Functional age
option_d: Social age
rationale: Functional age is the age associated with a person's job, role, and responsibilities. The woman's sense of identity was strongly tied to her professional role, and retirement has removed this role. Her feeling "invisible" because colleagues no longer seek her input reflects a disruption in her functional age — the age associated with her professional responsibilities and status.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: In a research study, participants aged 60–80 are asked "How old do you feel most of the time?" The responses range from 40 to 75, with most participants reporting feeling younger than their actual age. Which of Kastenbaum's types of age is this research question designed to measure?
option_a: Chronological age
option_b: Biological age
option_c: Subjective age
option_d: Social age
rationale: Subjective age is defined as how old a person actually feels themselves to be. Asking participants how old they feel most of the time directly measures their subjective age. Kastenbaum's framework validates that people often report feeling or acting older or younger than their years, making subjective age an important component of the aging experience.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A human resources manager at a technology company is reviewing promotion policies. She notices that employees over 50 are rarely promoted to leadership roles despite strong performance reviews, while younger employees with less experience are frequently advanced. The manager decides to implement age-blind promotion reviews. Which concept from Kastenbaum's framework best explains why this policy change is justified?
option_a: Chronological age does not determine functional capability
option_b: Biological age is the only reliable indicator of work performance
option_c: Social age determines promotion suitability
option_d: Subjective age varies too much to be useful
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework shows that individuals coexist with several ages, and chronological age (how many years lived) does not necessarily determine functional age (the age associated with one's job, role, and responsibilities). Employees over 50 may have functional capabilities equal to or greater than younger colleagues. The policy change recognises that chronological age alone is a poor proxy for professional capability.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher finds that in a sample of adults aged 50–80, subjective age predicts life satisfaction better than chronological age. Which inference from Kastenbaum's framework best explains this finding?
option_a: Chronological age is irrelevant to psychological well-being
option_b: How old people feel (subjective age) may be more psychologically meaningful than how many years they have lived
option_c: Biological age is the most important predictor of happiness
option_d: Social age has no relationship with life satisfaction
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework validates that older adulthood is a concept interpreted differently by different groups and helps researchers understand the subjective experience of aging. Subjective age — how old a person actually feels — captures the individual's internal experience of aging, which may be more psychologically meaningful than the objective count of years lived (chronological age). This explains why feeling younger than one's years correlates with greater life satisfaction.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Two individuals, both chronologically 65, are being assessed for a volunteer programme. Person A has severe arthritis, limited mobility, and feels "old and useless." Person B runs daily, has excellent health markers, and describes herself as "middle-aged." A programme coordinator suggests rejecting Person A based on chronological age alone. Which critique of this decision best applies Kastenbaum's framework?
option_a: The coordinator should use only biological age for all decisions
option_b: The decision ignores that both individuals share the same chronological age but differ dramatically in biological and subjective ages
option_c: Person A should be accepted because subjective age is the only important factor
option_d: The coordinator is correct because chronological age is the most objective measure
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework demonstrates that individuals coexist with several ages rather than a single numerical age. Both individuals are chronologically 65, but they differ dramatically in biological age (Person A has severe arthritis and limited mobility; Person B runs daily with excellent health) and subjective age (Person A feels "old and useless"; Person B describes herself as "middle-aged"). Using chronological age alone ignores these critical differences.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material uses the example of Mick Jagger being featured on the cover of Saga magazine (a magazine for retired people) to illustrate a concept. Which aspect of Kastenbaum's framework does this example most clearly demonstrate?
option_a: Chronological age is the only accurate measure of who belongs in a retirement magazine
option_b: The gap between chronological age and how a person is perceived or functions (functional/social age)
option_c: Biological age is irrelevant to public perception
option_d: Subjective age is culturally universal
rationale: The example demonstrates that Mick Jagger, despite being chronologically old enough to appear in a retirement magazine, was perceived as not fitting that category — to the point that his team was "horrified" at the photograph's use. This supports the view that adulthood and older adulthood are concepts interpreted differently by different groups, highlighting the gap between chronological age and how a person is perceived or functions (functional/social age).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A health psychologist develops two wellness programmes: Programme X targets participants based on their chronological age (e.g., all adults 65+), while Programme Y assesses biological, functional, and subjective ages to create individualised plans. Based on Kastenbaum's framework, which critique of Programme X is most valid?
option_a: Programme X is superior because chronological age is objective and easy to measure
option_b: Programme X may be ineffective because it assumes all 65-year-olds have the same biological, functional, and subjective ages
option_c: Programme Y is too complex and should not be used
option_d: Both programmes are equally effective
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework shows that individuals coexist with several ages, and these ages can diverge significantly. Assuming all 65-year-olds have the same needs based on chronological age ignores variations in biological age (physical condition), functional age (role and responsibilities), and subjective age (how old one feels). Programme Y's individualised approach better accounts for this multidimensionality.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher proposes that subjective age should replace chronological age as the standard measure for all gerontological research, arguing that how old people feel is more meaningful than how many years they have lived. Which is the strongest evaluation of this proposal based on Kastenbaum's framework?
option_a: The proposal is sound because subjective age captures the lived experience of aging better than any other measure
option_b: The proposal is too narrow because Kastenbaum's framework shows that all five ages contribute to understanding aging; replacing one with another ignores the multidimensionality of the concept
option_c: The proposal should be rejected because subjective age cannot be measured reliably
option_d: The proposal is unnecessary because chronological and subjective age are always the same
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework posits that individuals coexist with several ages — chronological, biological, subjective, functional, and social — not that one type should replace another. Each type of age contributes a different perspective on the aging experience. Replacing chronological age with subjective age would ignore biological realities, functional capabilities, and social contexts that are also essential to understanding aging comprehensively.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A fitness company markets an exercise programme exclusively to adults aged 50+, claiming it is "designed for the biological realities of aging." Critics argue that the programme should instead be marketed based on actual physical capacity. Which evaluation best aligns with Kastenbaum's framework?
option_a: The company is correct because chronological age always predicts biological capacity
option_b: The critics have a valid point because biological age (physical capacity) and chronological age can diverge significantly; some 50-year-olds may function like 30-year-olds while others function like 70-year-olds
option_c: The company should market only to people who feel old
option_d: Both the company and critics are wrong because functional age is the only relevant factor
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework demonstrates that biological age (how old the body seems physically) and chronological age (how many years lived) are distinct and can diverge significantly. People age at different rates due to hereditary endowments, socioeconomic backgrounds, and patterns of living. Marketing based on chronological age alone assumes a uniform biological reality that does not exist across all individuals of the same age.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Some employers have begun using "functional age assessments" alongside chronological age in workforce planning, arguing that these assessments provide a better measure of an employee's capacity to perform their role. Which is the strongest argument against relying primarily on functional age in employment decisions?
option_a: Functional age cannot be measured reliably
option_b: Functional age may fluctuate with role changes and does not capture the full aging picture as comprehensively as considering all five of Kastenbaum's ages together
option_c: Chronological age is always a better predictor of job performance
option_d: Functional age is not part of Kastenbaum's original framework
rationale: Kastenbaum's framework includes five coexisting ages: chronological, biological, subjective, functional, and social. Functional age is the age associated with a person's job or role, which may change with promotions, demotions, or reassignments. Relying primarily on functional age ignores biological limitations, subjective well-being, and social context that also affect work performance. The framework suggests that all five ages contribute to understanding an individual's aging experience.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A retirement community requires all residents to be chronologically 65 or older but organises activities based on residents' self-reported "energy levels" and interests rather than age. Which evaluation best reflects how this policy applies Kastenbaum's framework?
option_a: The policy is inconsistent because it uses chronological age for admission but ignores it for programming
option_b: The policy wisely recognises that within a group sharing the same chronological age, there is wide variation in subjective and functional ages
option_c: The policy should eliminate the chronological age requirement entirely
option_d: The policy should organise activities strictly by age groups
rationale: The admission requirement acknowledges chronological age as a legal/structural boundary, while activity programming based on energy levels and interests recognises Kastenbaum's insight that individuals of the same chronological age can have widely different subjective ages (how they feel) and functional ages (what they can do). This dual approach respects both the administrative necessity of age thresholds and the reality of individual variation in the aging experience.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A gerontologist argues that since Kastenbaum's types of age have "face validity," they should be considered scientifically validated measures of aging. Which is the strongest critique of this argument?
option_a: Face validity means the concept appears reasonable on the surface, but it does not constitute rigorous scientific validation
option_b: Kastenbaum's framework has been rejected by modern gerontologists
option_c: The types of age are too subjective to ever be scientifically validated
option_d: Face validity is the strongest form of scientific evidence
rationale: Face validity refers to whether a concept appears to measure what it claims to measure on the surface. While the course material notes that Kastenbaum's concept has face validity because many people report feeling or acting older or younger than their years, face validity alone does not constitute rigorous scientific validation. A concept with face validity still requires empirical testing for reliability, construct validity, and predictive validity before being considered scientifically validated.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)

option_d: The resistance is typical because the parent-child tie predates the blended family, and the stepparent entered as an outsider without a pre-existing warm bond
rationale: The course material states that since the parent-child tie predates the blended family, the stepparent enters as an outsider. Without a warm attachment bond to build on, discipline is usually ineffective. Too often, stepparents move into their new role too quickly. This understanding normalises the stepchildren's resistance and guides the therapist to focus on relationship-building rather than expecting automatic acceptance.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A gay couple adopts a 5-year-old child. Their extended family is initially unsupportive. Based on the course material, which outcome is most likely if the couple follows the patterns described in the research?
option_a: The child will definitely develop psychological problems
option_b: They will be forced to return the child
option_c: The extended family will permanently disown them
option_d: The couple will likely build a "family of choice" through friends, and over time interactions with their families of origin may become more positive
rationale: The course material states that when extended-family members have difficulty accepting homosexual parents, gay and lesbian parents often build "families of choice" through friends who assume the roles of relatives. It also notes that most of the time, parents of gays and lesbians cannot endure a permanent rift, and with time, interactions between homosexual parents and their families of origin become more positive and supportive.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher compares three family structures: (1) first-marriage families, (2) blended families where only one parent has prior children, and (3) blended families where both parents have prior children. She finds that family conflict increases across these three groups. Which analysis best explains this pattern using the course material?
option_a: Children in first-marriage families are always better behaved
option_b: Financial stress is the only factor affecting conflict levels
option_c: First-marriage families have no conflict whatsoever
option_d: Opportunities for conflict multiply as the number of pre-existing parent-child relationships increases, with both-partner prior children creating the most complex dynamics
rationale: The course material states that relationship quality is poorer when both adults have children from prior marriages than when only one does, because of more opportunities for conflict. Each pre-existing parent-child relationship brings its own dynamics, expectations, and loyalties. When both partners bring children, the potential for disagreement about child-rearing, discipline, and family roles increases exponentially.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material notes that fierce prejudice against homosexual parents still prevails in some areas despite research showing they are equally effective parents. Which analysis best explains this persistent prejudice?
option_a: The prejudice is justified because homosexual parents are less effective
option_b: Research about homosexual parenting has been inconclusive
option_c: The prejudice is based on empirical evidence of harm to children
option_d: The prejudice persists despite research evidence, suggesting it stems from cultural attitudes rather than scientific findings about parenting quality
rationale: The course material explicitly states that gay and lesbian parents are as committed to and effective at child-rearing as heterosexual parents, and their children are as well-adjusted as other children. It also notes that fierce prejudice still prevails in some areas. This disconnect between research evidence and persistent attitudes suggests the prejudice stems from cultural and social attitudes rather than from scientific findings about actual parenting quality or child outcomes.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A sociologist observes that children of never-married single mothers in low-SES environments show more antisocial behaviour than children in low-SES first-marriage families. Which analysis best distinguishes between correlation and causation in this scenario?
option_a: Marriage automatically prevents all antisocial behaviour in children
option_b: The antisocial behaviour is caused solely by genetics
option_c: The single-parent status directly causes antisocial behaviour regardless of SES
option_d: The absence of a father combined with low-SES conditions may correlate with antisocial behaviour, but strengthening social support and economic opportunities could mitigate these risks
rationale: The course material notes that children of never-married mothers who lack fatherly involvement are poor in school and display more antisocial behaviour than children in low-SES, first-marriage families. Crucially, it recommends strengthening social support, education, and employment opportunities for low-SES parents to help these families. This implies that the correlation between single parenthood and antisocial behaviour is mediated by socioeconomic factors and lack of support, not caused solely by family structure.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material describes how stepparents often criticise biological parents for being too lenient, while biological parents view stepparents as too harsh. Which underlying psychological mechanism best explains this common conflict pattern?
option_a: Biological parents are always too lenient
option_b: Stepparents are always too harsh by nature
option_c: Stepparents inherently dislike their stepchildren
option_d: The lack of a pre-existing warm attachment bond means stepparents lack the emotional foundation for patient, nuanced discipline, while biological parents are protective of their existing relationship with the child
rationale: The course material states that since the parent-child tie predates the blended family, the stepparent enters as an outsider without a warm attachment bond to build on. This lack of pre-existing bond means stepparents may approach discipline differently — without the patience and nuanced understanding that comes from a long-standing relationship. Biological parents, protective of their existing bond with the child, may view the stepparent's approach as too harsh. This dynamic explains the recurring conflict pattern.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher finds that children of lesbian mothers regard their mother's partner as "very much a parent." Which implication of this finding most strongly supports the argument that family functioning depends on relationship quality rather than family structure?
option_a: Mother's partners always replace biological fathers
option_b: Legal definitions of parenthood are irrelevant
option_c: The family structure determines all child outcomes
option_d: Children recognise parental figures based on the quality of care and relationship, not solely on biological or legal ties
rationale: The finding that children of lesbian mothers regard their mother's partner as "very much a parent" demonstrates that children recognise parental figures based on the quality of care, involvement, and relationship rather than solely on biological or legal ties. This supports the broader argument that family functioning and child adjustment depend on relationship quality rather than on the specific structure of the family.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material contrasts the legal history of custody cases involving gay parents with current practice. Which analysis best captures the shift described?
option_a: Courts have moved from assuming homosexuals could not be adequate parents (leading to loss of custody) to recognising that sexual orientation is irrelevant to custody decisions
option_b: Courts have always favoured gay parents in custody cases
option_c: There has been no change in custody law regarding gay parents
option_d: Gay parents are now automatically given custody regardless of other factors
rationale: The course material describes a significant shift: in the past, laws assuming that homosexuals could not be adequate parents led those who divorced a heterosexual partner to lose custody of their children. Today, sexual orientation is irrelevant to custody. This represents a movement from prejudice-based assumptions to evidence-based decision-making in family court.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A family educator designs a programme to help blended families develop connectedness. Based on the course material, which factor should she identify as the most significant barrier to connectedness?
option_a: The income level of the family
option_b: The education level of the parents
option_c: The age of the children
option_d: The fact that it generally takes 3 to 5 years for blended families to develop the connectedness and comfort of intact biological families, due to the stepparent entering as an outsider without pre-existing bonds
rationale: The course material explicitly states that it generally takes 3 to 5 years for blended families to develop the connectedness and comfort of intact biological families. This is because the stepparent enters the family as an outsider without a pre-existing warm attachment bond to build on. Family life education, couples counselling, and group therapy can help divorced and remarried adults adapt to the complexities of their new circumstances.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material states that "families of choice" are often built by homosexual parents when extended-family members have difficulty accepting them. Which social-emotional function does this phenomenon serve?
option_a: It indicates that homosexual parents have rejected all family values
option_b: It has no functional purpose
option_c: It replaces the biological family permanently
option_d: It provides social support and fulfils the need for kinship networks that the biological family may not provide
rationale: When extended-family members have difficulty accepting homosexual parents, gay and lesbian parents often build "families of choice" through friends who assume the roles of relatives. This serves the social-emotional function of providing support networks and fulfilling the human need for kinship and community. The course material also notes that with time, interactions between homosexual parents and their families of origin typically become more positive, suggesting "families of choice" serve as bridges rather than permanent replacements.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A counsellor compares the challenges faced by stepparents, never-married single parents, and gay/lesbian parents. Which common thread across all three groups does the course material support?
option_a: Only stepparents face real challenges
option_b: All three groups should be discouraged from parenting
option_c: All three groups are inherently less capable parents
option_d: Each group faces unique social and relational challenges that can impact parenting competence and psychological well-being
rationale: The course material states that diverse family forms result in varied styles of parenthood, and each type of family presents unique challenges to parenting competence and adult psychological well-being. Stepparents enter as outsiders without attachment bonds, never-married single parents may lack social and economic support in low-SES environments, and gay/lesbian parents may face fierce prejudice. The common thread is that each group faces unique challenges, not that any group is inherently less capable.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A politician claims that the government should provide no support to single mothers because "children need both a mother and a father to develop properly." Which is the strongest evidence-based evaluation of this claim using the course material?
option_a: The claim oversimplifies — while fatherly involvement matters, especially in low-SES contexts, the material recommends strengthening support for single-mother families rather than withholding it
option_b: The claim is irrelevant to child development
option_c: All children of single mothers are destined to fail
option_d: The claim is fully supported because children of single mothers always have problems
rationale: While the course material notes that children of never-married mothers who lack father involvement in low-SES environments show more adjustment problems, it does not claim this is inevitable or that single mothers are inherently unfit. Instead, it explicitly recommends strengthening social support, education, and employment opportunities for low-SES parents to help unmarried-mother families. This suggests that support — not withdrawal of support — is the evidence-based approach.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A school district is considering banning all discussion of diverse family structures in classrooms, arguing that only "traditional" families should be represented. Which is the strongest evaluation of this policy based on the course material?
option_a: The policy has no impact on any children
option_b: Non-traditional families are not part of the community
option_c: The policy is appropriate because only traditional families exist
option_d: The policy would harm children from non-traditional families by making them invisible and unsupported, despite research showing they can be equally well-adjusted
rationale: The course material documents that diverse family forms — stepparents, never-married single parents, and gay/lesbian parents — are increasingly common and that children in these families can be as well-adjusted as children in traditional families. Banning discussion of diverse family structures would erase the reality of these children's lives, deny them representation, and fail to address the unique challenges they may face, including social stigma.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A therapist argues that because blended families face so many documented challenges, couples considering remarriage with children should be discouraged from marrying. Which is the strongest evaluation of this recommendation?
option_a: Remarriage should always be encouraged regardless of circumstances
option_b: Children never adjust to blended families
option_c: The recommendation is well-supported because blended families always fail
option_d: The recommendation is too extreme — while challenges are real, family life education, couples counselling, and group therapy can help blended families succeed
rationale: The course material acknowledges that blended families face unique challenges including stepparents entering as outsiders, ineffective discipline, and conflicts between stepparents and biological parents. However, it also states that family life education, couples counselling, and group therapy can help divorced and remarried adults adapt to the complexities of their new circumstances. Discouraging remarriage entirely ignores the potential for successful intervention and adaptation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher concludes from her study that "gay fathers are better parents than heterosexual fathers" because they are more consistent in setting limits and more responsive to children's needs. Which critique of this conclusion is most valid?
option_a: The conclusion overgeneralises — the material mentions "some evidence suggests" this pattern "perhaps because" of less traditional gender identity, which is a tentative finding, not a definitive superiority claim
option_b: Gay fathers are never responsive to children's needs
option_c: Heterosexual fathers always set poor limits
option_d: The conclusion is fully supported by all research
rationale: The course material states that "some evidence suggests" gay fathers may be more consistent in setting limits and more responsive, and offers a tentative explanation ("perhaps because" of less traditional gender identity). This cautious, qualified language does not support a definitive claim that gay fathers are "better" parents. The material's overall conclusion is that gay and lesbian parents are "as committed to and effective at child-rearing as heterosexual parents" — emphasising equality, not superiority.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A blended family has been struggling for two years with stepparent-stepchild relationships. The biological parent says "I thought things would be better by now." Which evaluation best applies the course material's timeline to this situation?
option_a: Blended families never develop connectedness
option_b: The timeline is irrelevant to blended family adjustment
option_c: Two years is sufficient for full adjustment; the family is failing
option_d: The family's experience is within the normal range, as it generally takes 3 to 5 years for blended families to develop connectedness comparable to intact biological families
rationale: The course material explicitly states that it generally takes 3 to 5 years for blended families to develop the connectedness and comfort of intact biological families. Being only two years into the process, this family's struggles are within the expected adjustment period. This normalisation can help the family understand that their experience is typical rather than indicative of failure.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A judge in a custody case states that "children need a male and female role model, so lesbian couples should not have custody." Which is the strongest evaluation of this reasoning based on the course material?
option_a: Children of lesbian mothers always lack role models
option_b: The reasoning is scientifically valid
option_c: The reasoning contradicts the material's findings that children of lesbian mothers are as well-adjusted as other children and regard their mother's partner as very much a parent
option_d: The reasoning is supported by cultural norms in all societies
rationale: The course material states that children of lesbian mothers are as well-adjusted as other children, regard their mother's partner as very much a parent, and that the large majority of children in homosexual families are heterosexual. This directly contradicts the judge's assumption that children of lesbian couples are disadvantaged in terms of role models or adjustment.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A community programme offers support only to "traditional" two-parent biological families, arguing that non-traditional families have chosen their circumstances and do not need assistance. Which is the strongest evaluation of this approach?
option_a: Only traditional families experience challenges
option_b: The approach is fair because non-traditional families always have more resources
option_c: The approach is flawed because the material notes that diverse family forms present unique challenges and that support (social support, education, employment opportunities, counselling) benefits all family types
option_d: Non-traditional families never need support
rationale: The course material states that each type of family presents unique challenges to parenting competence and adult psychological well-being. It recommends support interventions for various non-traditional families: strengthening social support for single mothers, counselling for blended families, and acknowledging the prejudice faced by gay/lesbian parents. Excluding non-traditional families from community programmes ignores documented challenges and evidence-based support needs.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher publishes a study showing that children in blended families have slightly lower academic scores on average than children in intact first-marriage families. A journalist writes a headline: "Stepparenting Harms Children's Academic Success." Which is the strongest evaluation of this headline?
option_a: The headline is supported by all research on blended families
option_b: The headline accurately captures the study's findings
option_c: The headline is misleading because it confuses correlation with causation and ignores the course material's emphasis on process factors (gradual relationship building, family education, counselling) that can help blended families succeed
option_d: The headline should be celebrated as important news
rationale: While the study may have found a correlation between blended family status and slightly lower academic scores, the headline "Stepparenting Harms Children's Academic Success" implies a direct causal harm. The course material emphasises that blended families face unique process challenges (stepparents entering as outsiders, lack of attachment bonds) and that family life education, couples counselling, and group therapy can help them adapt. The headline ignores these modifiable process factors and stigmatises an entire family structure rather than addressing the specific challenges that can be overcome with support.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: How does post-formal thinking differ from Piaget's formal operational thinking when solving real-world problems?
option_a: There is no difference between the two
option_b: Post-formal thinking relies solely on logic, while formal operational thinking integrates emotion
option_c: Post-formal thinking involves making decisions based on situational constraints and integrating emotion with logic, while formal operations seeks single correct solutions
option_d: Post-formal thinking is only used by scientists, while formal operational thinking is used by everyone
rationale: Post-formal thinking is characterised by the objective use of practical common sense to deal with unclear problems, making decisions based on situational constraints and integrating emotion with logic to form context-dependent principles. This contrasts with formal operational thinking, which assumes a single correct solution and is geared toward closed-system, well-defined logical problems. Formal operations overemphasises pure logic and is suited for scientific problems with controllable variables.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A middle-aged manager faces a workplace conflict where two valued employees have irreconcilable but equally valid perspectives. Instead of demanding a single resolution, she develops a compromise that acknowledges both viewpoints and changes as circumstances change. Which characteristic of post-formal thought does this demonstrate?
option_a: Demand for a single correct solution
option_b: Reliance on absolute logical principles
option_c: Contextual problem solving by creating new principles based on changing circumstances
option_d: Rejection of all compromise
rationale: Post-formal thinkers solve problems by continuously creating new principles based on changing circumstances instead of applying set principles to all contexts. The manager's ability to develop a compromise that acknowledges multiple valid perspectives and adapts to changing situations demonstrates this contextual approach to problem solving, a hallmark of post-formal thought.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A teenager insists that there is one correct answer to a complex social issue and becomes frustrated when adults offer nuanced, context-dependent perspectives. Which cognitive limitation of formal operational thinking best explains the teenager's position?
option_a: Formal operations underemphasises the pragmatic quality of real-life cognitive activity and is geared for closed-system problems with single correct solutions
option_b: Teenagers lack the capacity for any logical reasoning
option_c: Formal operations integrates emotion with logic effectively
option_d: Teenagers always prefer complex answers
rationale: The course material lists several criticisms of formal operations: it overemphasises pure logic, underemphasises the pragmatic quality of real-life cognitive activity, and is geared for closed-system, well-defined problems that imply single correct solutions. Real life involves unlimited numbers of uncontrollable, changeable, and ill-defined variables. The teenager's insistence on one correct answer reflects this limitation of formal operational thinking.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material notes that formal operations "does not recognise the relative nature of knowledge and the need to adopt multiple frames of reference." Which characteristic of post-formal thought directly addresses this limitation?
option_a: Relativistic thinking — possessing an understanding of the relative, non-absolute nature of knowledge
option_b: Absolutist thinking — believing in single correct answers
option_c: Avoidance of multiple perspectives
option_d: Dependence on concrete operational thought
rationale: Relativistic thinking is listed as a key characteristic of post-formal thought: post-formal thinkers possess an understanding of the relative, non-absolute nature of knowledge. This directly addresses the limitation of formal operations, which does not recognise the relative nature of knowledge. Post-formal thinkers also engage in dialectic reasoning, synthesising contradictory thoughts and accepting contradiction as a basic aspect of reality.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: An educator proposes teaching post-formal thinking skills to adolescents to replace formal operational training. Which is the strongest evaluation of this proposal based on the course material?
option_a: The proposal is problematic because post-formal thinking builds on formal operations, begins late in adolescence, and culminates in older adulthood — it cannot simply replace earlier cognitive foundations
option_b: Formal operational thinking should never be taught
option_c: Adolescents are cognitively incapable of post-formal thinking
option_d: This is an excellent idea because post-formal thinking is superior at all ages
rationale: The course material states that post-formal thinking begins late in adolescence and culminates in the practical wisdom associated with older adulthood. It is a more complex stage that builds upon formal operational foundations. Adolescents need formal operational skills as a foundation before they can develop post-formal thinking. The proposal to replace formal operational training ignores this developmental sequence.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: What is the climacteric in relation to menopause?
option_a: A 10- to 15-year period during which the ovaries gradually stop producing eggs and sex hormones, preceding menopause
option_b: The immediate cessation of all hormone production
option_c: A psychological disorder associated with aging
option_d: A medical treatment for menopause symptoms
rationale: Menopause is the permanent ending of menstruation in women. It is usually preceded by the climacteric, a 10- to 15-year period during which the ovaries gradually stop producing eggs and sex hormones. Perimenopause encompasses this period of changing ovarian activity and also the first few years without menstrual cycling.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why might women in cultures that value the wisdom of older women view menopause more positively than women in cultures where women's roles are primarily reproductive?
option_a: Because hormonal changes are different across cultures
option_b: Because menopause does not occur in those cultures
option_c: Because they experience fewer physical symptoms
option_d: Because cultural values shape the meaning of menopause — in cultures valuing older women's wisdom, menopause is seen as a positive life event, while in reproductive-focused cultures, the inability to bear children represents a loss of status
rationale: Attitudes towards menopause vary depending on cultural connotations. In societies where women's role is mostly reproductive, the inability to bear more children is a loss of status. In cultures in which the wisdom and experience of older women is valued, menopause is seen as a positive life event. Cultural values fundamentally shape how menopause is experienced and interpreted.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
