stem: A 52-year-old woman reports experiencing hot flashes, mood swings, vaginal dryness, and irregular menstrual periods over the past year. Her physician explains these are related to changing ovarian activity. Which term best describes what this woman is experiencing?
option_a: Post-menopausal syndrome
option_b: Climacteric completion
option_c: Premature menopause
option_d: Perimenopause — the period of changing ovarian activity characterised by hormonal and physical changes
rationale: Perimenopause encompasses the period of changing ovarian activity and the first few years without menstrual cycling, typically characterised by hormonal and physical changes including hot flashes, mood swings, vaginal changes, and irregular periods. Menopause is only confirmed when a woman has not experienced a menstrual cycle for one full year.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A healthcare provider in a multicultural clinic is developing educational materials about menopause. Which approach best reflects the course material's findings about cultural variations in menopause attitudes?
option_a: Acknowledge that attitudes toward menopause vary by culture and individual expectations, and present both positive and negative perspectives
option_b: Tell all women that menopause is always a positive experience
option_c: Avoid discussing menopause because it is culturally sensitive
option_d: Present menopause as a uniformly negative experience for all women
rationale: The course material notes that attitudes towards menopause vary depending on cultural connotations and women's individual expectations. In general, young women and men view menopause more negatively, whereas women who have gone through the experience view it more positively. Some women mourn the loss of youth, while others are glad to be rid of menstruation. A culturally sensitive approach acknowledges this variation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Which of the following is identified in the course material as a psychological issue contributing to sexual dysfunction, where an individual observes and judges their own sexual performance rather than experiencing intimacy?
option_a: Projection
option_b: Performance anxiety
option_c: Spectatoring
option_d: Displacement
rationale: Spectatoring is a psychological issue contributing to sexual dysfunction where an individual observes and judges their own sexual performance rather than experiencing the intimacy. The course material lists spectatoring alongside other psychological factors such as anxiety, interfering thoughts, lack of communication, insufficient stimulation, and relationship conflicts as contributors to sexual dysfunction.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A middle-aged man reports difficulty maintaining an erection during sexual activity. His physician rules out organic causes and discovers the man is preoccupied with whether he is satisfying his partner, often mentally monitoring his own performance during intimacy. Which diagnosis best fits this description?
option_a: Erectile dysfunction with contributing factor of spectatoring
option_b: Vaginismus
option_c: Dyspareunia
option_d: Premature ejaculation
rationale: Erectile dysfunction refers to the inability of a man to have or maintain an erection. The course material identifies spectatoring (observing and judging one's own sexual performance) as a psychological issue contributing to dysfunction. The man's preoccupation with mentally monitoring his own performance during intimacy is a clear example of spectatoring contributing to erectile dysfunction.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A couple in their 50s reports that sexual intercourse has become painful for the woman. The woman had been sexually active throughout her marriage but now experiences vaginal dryness and thinning vaginal walls. A physician suggests hormone-replacement therapy. Which condition is most likely being treated?
option_a: Female orgasmic dysfunction
option_b: Premature ejaculation
option_c: Vaginismus
option_d: Dyspareunia related to decreased estrogen during the climacteric
rationale: Dyspareunia refers to painful intercourse in women or men. The woman's symptoms (vaginal dryness, thinning vaginal walls) are consistent with decreased estrogen levels during the climacteric/perimenopause period. The course material notes that decreased estrogen leads to thinning of vaginal walls, shrinking of the vagina, and decreased vaginal lubrication, which can cause pain during intercourse. Hormone-replacement therapy addresses these estrogen-related changes.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material states that sexual dysfunctions may be caused by physical problems, medications, alcohol, or psychological factors. A therapist sees a patient with erectile dysfunction who drinks heavily, takes blood pressure medication, and reports severe work-related anxiety. Which analytical approach best fits the course material's perspective?
option_a: The dysfunction is purely psychological
option_b: Only physical factors matter in middle-aged men
option_c: The dysfunction is caused solely by the alcohol
option_d: The dysfunction likely results from an interaction of multiple factors — physical (medication), behavioural (alcohol), and psychological (anxiety) — requiring a comprehensive assessment
rationale: The course material states that sexual dysfunctions may be caused by physical problems such as fatigue or illness, the use of prescription medications, other drugs, or alcohol, or psychological factors including anxiety, interfering thoughts, spectatoring, and relationship conflicts. A comprehensive assessment must consider the interaction of biological, behavioural, and psychological factors rather than attributing dysfunction to a single cause.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to Erikson, what is the primary developmental task of middle adulthood?
option_a: Identity versus role confusion
option_b: Achieving intimacy versus isolation
option_c: Generativity versus stagnation
option_d: Ego integrity versus despair
rationale: Erikson (1968) proposed that middle-aged adults face the issue of generativity versus stagnation, which is the seventh stage in his life-span theory. Generativity encompasses adults' desire to leave legacies of themselves to the next generation. By contrast, stagnation (sometimes called "self-absorption") develops when individuals sense that they have done nothing for the next generation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 48-year-old teacher mentors new teachers, volunteers at a community youth centre, and is writing a book about educational philosophy. Which form of generativity is she primarily demonstrating across these activities?
option_a: Only biological and parental generativity
option_b: Only biological generativity
option_c: Parental, work, and cultural generativity
option_d: Only stagnation
rationale: The course material identifies four forms of generativity: biological (having offspring), parental (nurturing and guiding children), work (developing skills passed down to others), and cultural (creating, renovating, or conserving some aspect of culture that survives). The teacher mentoring new teachers demonstrates work generativity, volunteering at a youth centre demonstrates parental generativity, and writing a book demonstrates cultural generativity.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A middle-aged executive has devoted his entire life to climbing the corporate ladder. He has no children, no mentees, no community involvement, and now feels his work is meaningless. He reports feeling self-absorbed and describes his life as "going nowhere." Which Eriksonian concept best describes his current state?
option_a: Stagnation — characterised by self-absorption and a lack of psychological movement or growth
option_b: Generativity through work alone
option_c: Successful resolution of the midlife crisis
option_d: Ego integrity
rationale: Stagnation (sometimes called "self-absorption") develops when individuals sense that they have done nothing for the next generation. The middle-aged adult who fails to develop generativity experiences stagnation with its associated self-indulgence. Those unable to cope with managing a household, raising children, or managing their career are likely to feel psychological stagnation. The executive's lack of connection to the next generation through any form of legacy fits this description.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A community organisation wants to promote generativity among its middle-aged members. Which programme would best align with Erikson's concept of generativity?
option_a: A mentoring programme where experienced members guide younger members in career development and community leadership
option_b: A programme encouraging members to focus exclusively on their own well-being
option_c: A competition for individual achievement awards
option_d: A programme focused solely on retirement planning
rationale: Generativity encompasses adults' desire to leave legacies of themselves to the next generation through biological, parental, work, and cultural forms. Adults promote and guide the next generation by parenting, teaching, leading, and doing things that benefit the community. A mentoring programme directly facilitates work generativity (passing down skills) and contributes to the continuation and improvement of society through connection to the next generation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to Levinson, what is the primary developmental task of the "early adult transition" (ages 17–22)?
option_a: Resolving the midlife crisis
option_b: Establishing retirement plans
option_c: Achieving peak career success
option_d: Transitioning from dependence to independence and forming a dream for one's career and marriage
rationale: Levinson described the early adult transition (ages 17–22) as a period of transitioning from dependence to independence, marked by the formation of a dream — an image of the kind of life the youth wants to have, especially in terms of a career and marriage. This is the first stage in his developmental theory spanning ages 17 to 65.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Levinson found that 70–80 percent of the men he interviewed experienced the midlife transition (ages 40–45) as tumultuous and psychologically painful. Which critique of his methodology best explains why this finding might not apply universally?
option_a: Levinson only interviewed middle-aged men, and the data about earlier stages relied on retrospective memory, which may be distorted
option_b: All men experience midlife exactly as Levinson described
option_c: Levinson's sample was too diverse
option_d: Midlife transitions do not exist
rationale: The course material notes that because Levinson interviewed middle-aged men, the data about middle adulthood can be considered more valid than the data about early adulthood. When individuals are asked to remember information about earlier parts of their lives, they may distort and forget things. Additionally, stage theories have been criticised for overemphasising the universal nature of midlife crises, with subsequent research finding extensive individual variation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material notes that stage theories like Levinson's are criticised for focusing on "universals" while not adequately addressing "individual variations." Which finding from subsequent research best supports this critique?
option_a: One extensive study of 500 men at midlife found extensive individual variation, with crises triggered by specific life events rather than just age
option_b: All men experience identical midlife crises
option_c: Stage theories have been completely disproven
option_d: Individual variation does not exist in adult development
rationale: The course material explicitly states that one extensive study of a random sample of 500 men at midlife found extensive individual variation. In the individual variations view, middle-aged adults interpret, shape, alter, and give meaning to their lives. Some individuals may experience a midlife crisis in some contexts but not others. Crises were found to be triggered by life events (job loss, financial problems, illness) rather than solely by reaching a particular age.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A self-help author writes a book claiming that "every man must experience a midlife crisis between ages 40 and 45 to achieve psychological growth." Which is the strongest evaluation of this claim based on the course material?
option_a: No men experience midlife crises
option_b: The claim is fully supported by Levinson's research
option_c: The claim overstates Levinson's findings by ignoring subsequent research showing extensive individual variation and that crises are triggered by events, not just age
option_d: The claim should be accepted because it helps sell books
rationale: While Levinson found that 70–80 percent of men he interviewed experienced the midlife transition as tumultuous, the course material notes that stage theories are criticised for overemphasising universals. Subsequent research found extensive individual variation, and in one-third of reported midlife crises, the crisis was triggered by life events (job loss, financial problems, illness) rather than just age. The claim that "every man must" experience a crisis overstates the universality and ignores this important nuance.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Some critics argue that Levinson's theory should be discarded entirely because it overemphasises crises and does not account for individual variation. Which is the most balanced evaluation of this critique?
option_a: The theory is perfect and has no limitations
option_b: Individual variation makes all stage theories completely worthless
option_c: The theory should be completely discarded because it has no value
option_d: While the theory has limitations in overemphasising universal crises, it remains valuable for describing the developmental tasks and transitions that many adults navigate, even if the experience varies
rationale: The course material presents both Levinson's contributions and the criticisms of his theory. While noting that stage theories overemphasise the universal nature of midlife crises and do not adequately address individual variation, the material also describes Levinson's stages and transitions in detail, suggesting they provide a useful framework for understanding potential developmental tasks. A balanced evaluation recognises both the value and the limitations.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: How does the contemporary life-events approach differ from the early version in explaining how life events influence development?
option_a: The contemporary approach considers the life event, mediating factors, adaptation, life-stage context, and socio-historical context, while the early version assumed events uniformly forced personality changes
option_b: The contemporary approach ignores life events entirely
option_c: There is no difference between the two versions
option_d: The early version was more sophisticated
rationale: The early version of the life-events approach viewed life events as uniformly taxing circumstances that forced personality changes without considering context. The contemporary approach is more sophisticated: how life events influence development depends not only on the event itself but also on mediating factors (physical health, family supports), the individual's adaptation (appraisal of threat, coping strategies), the life-stage context, and the socio-historical context.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 55-year-old woman with strong family support, good health, and financial stability loses her job. She views this as an opportunity to start her own business. A 55-year-old woman with poor health, no family support, and financial difficulties also loses her job. She views this as a devastating catastrophe. Which concept best explains why the same event produces such different responses?
option_a: Age alone determines the response to job loss
option_b: Job loss is never stressful
option_c: Both women should react identically to job loss
option_d: The contemporary life-events approach — the same event has different impacts depending on mediating factors (health, support, finances), adaptation (coping strategies and appraisal), and context
rationale: The contemporary life-events approach explains how the same event can have different impacts on different individuals. How life events influence development depends on mediating factors (physical health and family supports), the individual's adaptation (appraisal of the threat and coping strategies), the life-stage context, and the socio-historical context. One individual may perceive a life event as highly stressful, whereas another may perceive the same event as a challenge.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A therapist is working with a client whose spouse recently died. The client is 35 and has two young children. The therapist recognises that this death will likely have a different developmental impact than the same event occurring to a 75-year-old with grown children. Which aspect of the contemporary life-events approach does the therapist's reasoning reflect?
option_a: The life-stage context — the age at which the event occurs and the different developmental demands at that stage
option_b: The mediating factors only
option_c: The adaptation process only
option_d: The socio-historical context
rationale: The life-stage context is a key component of the contemporary life-events approach. The same event (spousal death) occurring at different life stages has different implications. A 35-year-old with young children faces different developmental demands (raising children, career building) than a 75-year-old with grown children. The course material gives the example that divorce may be more stressful after many years of marriage when adults are in their fifties than when in their twenties.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material contrasts the early and contemporary life-events approaches. Which analytical statement best captures the fundamental shift in how researchers understand the relationship between life events and personality development?
option_a: The shift moved from viewing events as deterministic forces to viewing their impact as moderated by multiple individual and contextual factors
option_b: There was no shift — both approaches are identical
option_c: The shift moved toward viewing events as irrelevant to development
option_d: The shift moved toward viewing personality as entirely genetically determined
rationale: The early approach viewed life events as taxing circumstances that uniformly forced personality changes. The contemporary approach recognises that the impact of life events is moderated by multiple factors: the event itself, mediating factors (health, support), individual adaptation (coping, appraisal), life-stage context, and socio-historical context. This represents a shift from deterministic thinking to a more nuanced, interactionist understanding.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher claims that the contemporary life-events approach has made stage theories like Levinson's obsolete. Which is the strongest evaluation of this claim?
option_a: Life-events approaches explain nothing about adult development
option_b: The claim is accurate — life-events approaches have completely replaced stage theories
option_c: The claim is overstated — both approaches offer complementary perspectives; stages describe developmental tasks while life-events explain how circumstances shape the experience of those tasks
option_d: Stage theories were never useful
rationale: The course material presents both stage theories (Levinson's seasons, Erikson's generativity versus stagnation) and the life-events approach as valid ways to examine adult personality development. Stage theories describe developmental tasks and transitions at different ages, while life-events approaches explain how specific circumstances shape the experience of development. These are complementary rather than competing perspectives.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why are middle-aged adults sometimes called "the sandwich generation"?
option_a: Because they are caught between the demands of caring for their own children and providing care for their aging parents
option_b: Because they typically eat most of their meals between work shifts
option_c: Because they are between two career stages
option_d: Because they live between two generations of family members
rationale: Middle-aged adults are sometimes called "the sandwich generation" because they are caught in the middle between the dual demands of caring for their own children and providing care for their aging parents. Healthier lifestyles, more efficient drugs, and improved technology have extended lifespan, meaning middle-aged people are often torn between the needs of their children and their elderly parents.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 50-year-old woman is assisting her elderly parents with food shopping, housekeeping, banking, transportation, and medical appointments while also supporting her teenage children through school and extracurricular activities. She reports feeling exhausted and overwhelmed. Which type of support would most directly address her situation based on the course material?
option_a: A recommendation to stop helping her parents
option_b: A suggestion to ignore her children's needs
option_c: Financial advice only
option_d: Support groups and counselling resources that teach caregiver skills and offer emotional support
rationale: The course material explicitly recommends that support groups and counselling resources are available for adults caring for their older parents. These forms of assistance typically provide information, teach caregiver skills, and offer emotional support. The woman's situation — managing dual caregiving responsibilities — is the classic sandwich generation experience that these supports are designed to address.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A human resources director at a large company notices increased absenteeism and reduced productivity among employees aged 45–60. Employee surveys reveal many are caring for both school-age children and aging parents. Which workplace intervention best addresses the "sandwich generation" challenge?
option_a: Early retirement incentives for all employees over 45
option_b: Mandatory overtime for all employees
option_c: Flexible work arrangements, employee assistance programmes, and referrals to support groups for caregiving employees
option_d: A policy forbidding employees from discussing family responsibilities
rationale: The sandwich generation faces dual caregiving demands that can affect work performance. Flexible work arrangements accommodate medical appointments and family needs. Employee assistance programmes can provide referrals to the support groups and counselling resources the course material recommends. This comprehensive approach acknowledges the reality of employees' caregiving responsibilities while maintaining productivity.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material notes that caring for aging parents can involve financial assistance, physical care, and emotional support, and that caregivers may experience negative feelings including anger, bargaining, depression, and eventual acceptance. Which psychological framework does this emotional progression most closely parallel?
option_a: Erikson's stage of trust versus mistrust
option_b: Maslow's hierarchy of needs
option_c: Kubler-Ross's stages of dying, adapted to the caregiver's experience of loss and adjustment
option_d: Piaget's stages of cognitive development
rationale: The course material describes caregivers experiencing denial, anger, bargaining with the health system for more care, depression about deteriorating health, and eventual acceptance — a progression that closely parallels Kubler-Ross's stages of dying. The caregiver is experiencing a form of grief and adjustment as they face the declining health and changing relationship with their parent.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A middle-aged father is expected to stay late at work to finish an important project on the same night as his daughter's school play. He cannot be in both places at once. Which concept does this situation best illustrate?
option_a: Role ambiguity
option_b: Role overload
option_c: Role conflict — when the demands and expectations of various roles directly clash
option_d: Role spillover
rationale: Role conflict occurs when the demands and expectations of various roles directly clash with each other. In this case, the worker role (stay late at work) conflicts with the parent role (attend daughter's school play). This is distinct from role overload (too many demands in the time allowed) and role spillover (preoccupations about one role interfering with another).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A middle-aged woman is so preoccupied with worries about her ill mother while at work that she cannot concentrate on her job assignments. Her supervisor notices declining performance. Which concept best describes this situation?
option_a: Workplace harassment
option_b: Role conflict
option_c: Role spillover — when preoccupations about one role interfere with the ability to carry out another
option_d: Role overload
rationale: Role spillover occurs when the demands or preoccupations about one role interfere with the ability to carry out another role. In this case, worries about the caregiving role (ill mother) spill over into and interfere with the work role (concentrating on job assignments). This is distinct from role conflict, which involves direct temporal or physical clashes between roles.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A middle-aged parent with three children (ages 8, 11, and 15) finds the demands of attending functions at three different schools, transporting children to various activities, and being emotionally available for daily problems to be completely exhausting. There are simply too many demands for the time available. Which concept applies?
option_a: Role overload — too many demands and expectations to handle in the time allowed
option_b: Role spillover
option_c: Role elimination
option_d: Role conflict
rationale: Role overload occurs as a result of having too many demands and expectations to handle in the time allowed. The parent's situation — multiple schools, various activities, emotional demands — represents an overwhelming quantity of role demands that exceed available time and energy, rather than a direct clash between specific roles (role conflict) or psychological preoccupation (role spillover).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material describes role overload, role conflict, and role spillover as leading to "reduced satisfaction at work and in family roles, and in a decline in the person's sense of well-being." Which analysis best explains the cumulative impact of all three on middle-aged adults?
option_a: The three concepts are interrelated — overload creates the conditions for conflict, and unresolved conflict leads to spillover, collectively eroding well-being across domains
option_b: Only one of the three can occur at a time
option_c: They have no impact on well-being
option_d: Each operates independently and never overlaps
rationale: The course material presents the three concepts as interrelated and collectively impactful. Role overload (too many demands) creates conditions where direct clashes between roles (role conflict) become more likely, and when demands cannot be reconciled, preoccupations from one role spill into another (role spillover). The combination of all three leads to reduced satisfaction at work and in family roles and a decline in overall well-being.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why is the empty nest period described as "far more traumatic for women than for men," particularly for women who devoted their adult years to homemaking?
option_a: Women do not value their relationships with their husbands
option_b: Women are inherently less capable of handling change
option_c: These women may experience "retirement shock" similar to men's retirement — their primary role has lessened and they may have few outside interests to fill their time
option_d: Men always enjoy the empty nest period
rationale: The course material states that the empty-nest period is far more traumatic for women than for men, especially for women who have devoted their adult years to homemaking and who have few interests or resources to fill their time when their homemaking jobs lessen or come to an end. Many experience a "retirement shock" similar to that experienced by men when they retire.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A marriage counsellor sees a couple who, after their last child left for college, find they have little in common and struggle to converse without discussing their children. The wife, who was a full-time homemaker, feels lost and unneeded. Which intervention best addresses their situation?
option_a: Tell the wife her feelings are invalid
option_b: Recommend immediate divorce
option_c: Help the couple re-establish their relationship as a "twosome" by exploring communication and lifestyle needs, recognising this as a common empty nest adjustment challenge
option_d: Suggest they have more children
rationale: The course material states that after years of living in a family-centered home, the "empty nest" requires adjustment to a pair-centered home. Husbands and wives often grew apart during child-rearing years. The challenge is to re-establish a relationship as a "twosome" by exploring communication and lifestyle needs. Failure to take on this challenge may lead to quarrels, sexual dysfunction, extramarital affairs, and divorce.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 55-year-old woman who devoted her life to raising four children experiences a sense of purposelessness and depression after the youngest leaves home. She has no hobbies, no career, and minimal social contacts outside her family. Which concept from the course material best describes her experience?
option_a: Clinical depression unrelated to life stage
option_b: Normal ageing process
option_c: Atypical reaction to the empty nest
option_d: "Retirement shock" — the traumatic experience for women whose primary role has suddenly lessened, especially when they lack outside interests
rationale: The course material specifically describes how the empty-nest period is far more traumatic for women who have devoted their adult years to homemaking and have few interests or resources to fill their time. Many experience a "retirement shock" similar to that experienced by men when they retire. This woman's situation — no hobbies, no career, minimal outside contacts — fits this description precisely.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material states that the absence of children as diversions during the empty nest period "may reveal how far apart spouses have grown." Which analysis best explains why children serve as "diversions" that mask marital problems?
option_a: Spouses never grow apart during child-rearing years
option_b: Children intentionally distract parents from marital problems
option_c: The shared focus on child-rearing activities and decisions provides common ground and daily interaction that may mask growing emotional distance between spouses
option_d: Children cause all marital problems
rationale: The course material notes that during the child-rearing years, husbands and wives often grew apart and developed individual interests. The shared focus on children provided common ground, daily interaction, and mutual purpose. When children leave, this common ground disappears, revealing the emotional distance that has developed. The challenge exists to re-establish a relationship as a "twosome" rather than as co-parents.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A cultural commentator claims that "the empty nest syndrome is a myth invented by feminists — most parents are thrilled when their children leave home." Which is the strongest evaluation of this claim based on the course material?
option_a: Only fathers experience empty nest difficulties
option_b: The claim is fully supported by research
option_c: The claim oversimplifies a complex reality — while some parents adjust well, the material documents that many find the transition traumatic, especially women who devoted their lives to homemaking and experience "retirement shock"
option_d: The empty nest is never difficult for anyone
rationale: The course material documents that while the empty nest is sometimes viewed simply as a period of freedom, it is frequently a traumatic adjustment, especially for women who lacked outside interests during the child-rearing years and experience "retirement shock." The material also notes that failure to navigate this transition can lead to quarrels, sexual dysfunction, and divorce. The claim that it is a "myth" ignores well-documented challenges.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Which statement about senility is most accurate according to the course material?
option_a: Senility only occurs after age 80
option_b: Senility is a specific disease that can be cured with medication
option_c: Senility is an inevitable and universal part of aging that affects all elderly people
option_d: Senility is a period during late adulthood when a more or less complete physical breakdown takes place alongside mental disorganisation, but it is not inevitable — some may never experience it
rationale: Senility refers to a period during late adulthood when a more or less complete physical breakdown takes place alongside mental disorganisation. An individual suffering from senility often becomes eccentric, careless, absentminded, and socially withdrawn. However, the course material explicitly states that senility is not an inevitable or universal part of aging — it may come as early as the fifties for some, while others may never experience it because they die before deterioration sets in.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: What are the two main categories of causes of senility identified in the course material?
option_a: Physical causes (changes in body cells and brain tissue due to aging) and psychological causes (unfavourable attitudes toward oneself, work, and life)
option_b: Lack of exercise and poor diet only
option_c: Infectious diseases and accidents
option_d: Genetic factors and environmental pollution
rationale: The course material identifies two main categories of causes of senility: physical causes — changes in the body cells and brain tissue caused by the aging process itself; and psychological causes — unfavourable attitudes toward oneself, other people, work, and life in general that can accelerate mental regression.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material distinguishes between the normal "slow and gradual" decline of late adulthood and the more severe state of senility. Which analysis best captures this distinction?
option_a: Senility is just another term for normal aging
option_b: All older adults eventually develop senility
option_c: There is no difference between normal aging and senility
option_d: Normal aging involves slow, gradual declines, while senility represents a more complete physical breakdown and mental disorganisation that is not inevitable
rationale: The course material distinguishes between the period during late adulthood when physical and mental decline is slow and gradual (normal aging) and senility, which refers to a more or less complete physical breakdown with mental disorganisation. It explicitly states that senility is not inevitable and that some individuals may never experience it because they die before deterioration sets in.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 68-year-old woman becomes somewhat forgetful and less socially active. Her family assumes she is becoming senile and begins making all decisions for her. A gerontologist evaluates her and finds her changes are consistent with normal, gradual aging rather than senility. Which analysis best explains the family's error?
option_a: Social withdrawal is always a sign of senility
option_b: The family correctly identified senility
option_c: The family confused normal, gradual aging declines with the more severe and complete breakdown characteristic of senility, and by taking over decisions, may have accelerated psychological decline through unfavourable attitudes
option_d: Forgetfulness is never normal in aging
rationale: The course material distinguishes between normal gradual declines and the more severe state of senility. It also notes that psychological causes of senility include unfavourable attitudes toward oneself and life. By assuming senility and taking over decisions, the family may have contributed to unfavourable attitudes that can accelerate mental regression, while the gerontologist recognised that the woman's changes were consistent with normal aging.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A geriatric care facility has two new residents: a 78-year-old former professor who remains physically active, socially engaged, and mentally sharp; and a 62-year-old former office worker who has experienced rapid physical deterioration, severe memory loss, and has become withdrawn and eccentric. Based on the course material, which statement best applies?
option_a: The 78-year-old must eventually develop senility because of her age
option_b: Occupation determines whether senility develops
option_c: The 62-year-old cannot be experiencing senility because he is too young
option_d: Senility may come as early as the fifties for some, while others may remain healthy and active well into their 70s, demonstrating individual differences in aging
rationale: The course material explicitly states that senility may come as early as the fifties for some, while others may never experience it because they die before deterioration sets in. It also emphasises that aging affects different people differently due to hereditary endowments, socioeconomic and educational backgrounds, and patterns of living. These two residents illustrate this individual variation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A nursing home administrator wants to create an environment that minimises the risk of psychological causes of senility. Which programme component would be most effective based on the course material?
option_a: Programmes that promote favourable attitudes toward oneself, meaningful activities, work, and life, including social engagement and continued purpose
option_b: Complete isolation to prevent stress
option_c: Removing all responsibilities from residents
option_d: An environment that restricts resident autonomy for their safety
rationale: The course material identifies psychological causes of senility as unfavourable attitudes toward oneself, other people, work, and life in general. An environment that promotes favourable attitudes through meaningful activities, social engagement, continued sense of purpose, and positive self-regard would directly address these psychological risk factors. Restricting autonomy or isolating residents could create the very unfavourable attitudes that contribute to senility.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A family physician is counselling the adult children of a 72-year-old who has begun showing signs of mild forgetfulness and occasional confusion. The children are terrified their parent is becoming senile. Which response best aligns with the course material?
option_a: "Mild forgetfulness can be part of normal aging; senility involves more complete breakdown and is not inevitable. Let's distinguish between normal decline and more serious concerns"
option_b: "Forgetfulness at 72 always indicates senility"
option_c: "There is nothing you can do either way"
option_d: "All older adults become senile eventually, so your fears are justified"
rationale: The course material distinguishes between normal gradual declines and the more severe state of senility, and explicitly states that senility is not inevitable. It emphasises individual differences in aging. The physician should reassure the family that mild forgetfulness can be part of normal aging while monitoring for more serious signs, rather than catastrophising normal age-related changes.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A wellness company markets an expensive "anti-senility" supplement claiming to "prevent all forms of mental decline in old age." Which is the strongest evaluation of this claim based on the course material?
option_a: Supplements are the only way to prevent senility
option_b: Everyone should take this supplement regardless of cost
option_c: The claim is fully supported by scientific evidence
option_d: The claim is misleading because senility has both physical causes (changes in body cells and brain tissue) and psychological causes (attitudes toward life); no supplement can address all these factors, and senility is not inevitable for everyone anyway
rationale: The course material identifies two categories of senility causes: physical changes in body cells and brain tissue, and psychological factors such as unfavourable attitudes. No single supplement could address both biological aging processes and psychological attitudes. Furthermore, since senility is not inevitable and some individuals never experience it, a universal prevention claim is scientifically unsupportable.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A policy maker proposes that all adults over 70 should be required to undergo annual cognitive testing and, if showing any decline, should be automatically assigned a legal guardian. Which is the strongest evaluation of this proposal based on the course material?
option_a: All adults over 70 need guardians
option_b: This is an excellent policy that will protect all elderly people
option_c: The proposal is overly broad and potentially harmful — it fails to distinguish between normal gradual decline and senility, ignores individual differences, and could create the unfavourable attitudes that contribute to psychological causes of decline
option_d: Cognitive decline never occurs before age 70
rationale: The course material emphasises individual differences in aging, distinguishes between normal gradual decline and senility, and notes that senility is not inevitable. It also identifies unfavourable attitudes toward oneself as a psychological cause of senility. A blanket policy could strip autonomous, competent older adults of their rights while potentially creating the very unfavourable attitudes that accelerate mental regression.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 75-year-old man has retired from a career he loved, lost his spouse, and now spends most days alone watching television. He has developed negative attitudes about his usefulness and shows signs of social withdrawal and mental slowing. A community worker wants to intervene. Which approach best addresses both physical and psychological factors?
option_a: Suggest he move to an isolated location
option_b: Recommend complete rest and inactivity
option_c: Connect him with social activities, meaningful volunteer work, and grief support to address unfavourable attitudes and promote engagement
option_d: Tell him his decline is inevitable
rationale: The course material identifies both physical causes (changes in body cells and brain tissue) and psychological causes (unfavourable attitudes toward oneself, work, and life) of senility. This man's situation — loss of spouse, retirement from meaningful work, isolation, negative self-attitudes — suggests psychological factors are significant. Social activities, meaningful work, and grief support would address the psychological contributors while maintaining physical and mental engagement.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to the course material, on which type of memory task do older adults perform almost as well as younger adults?
option_a: Tasks requiring spontaneous encoding strategies
option_b: Tasks requiring creating mental associations
option_c: Open-ended recall tasks
option_d: Recognition tasks
rationale: The course material explicitly states that while older adults have significant trouble recalling items on open-ended tasks, they perform almost as well as younger adults on recognition tasks. Older adults may be able to answer a multiple-choice question but not an open-ended one, and while they have more trouble recalling items than younger adults, they do about as well in recognising items they know.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why are encoding problems particularly significant for older adults' memory performance?
option_a: Older adults refuse to use encoding strategies
option_b: Encoding problems mean older adults cannot form any new memories
option_c: Older adults are less likely to spontaneously arrange material or create mental associations, which makes information harder to remember
option_d: Encoding problems only affect long-term memory
rationale: Encoding problems refer to decreased efficiency in spontaneously arranging material or creating mental associations. The course material notes that older people are less likely to spontaneously arrange material in alphabetical order or create mental associations. However, it also importantly notes that older adults can improve their encoding skills through training or instruction, which demonstrates to educators that memory and encoding skills can be actively improved.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A university offers continuing education courses for older adults. An instructor notices that students struggle to recall information from lectures but perform well on multiple-choice exams. Which explanation from the course material best accounts for this pattern?
option_a: The lectures are too difficult
option_b: Multiple-choice exams are always easier
option_c: The students are not paying attention in class
option_d: Older adults have more trouble with retrieval on open-ended recall tasks but perform about as well as younger adults on recognition tasks like multiple-choice questions
rationale: The course material explicitly states that in retrieving learned information from memory, older adults may be able to answer a multiple-choice question (recognition) but not an open-ended one (recall). While they have more trouble recalling items than younger adults, they do about as well in recognising items they know. This perfectly describes the students' performance pattern.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A memory trainer is designing a programme for older adults to improve their memory skills. Based on the course material, which strategy would be most effective?
option_a: Recommending memory-enhancing drugs exclusively
option_b: Telling older adults that memory cannot be improved after a certain age
option_c: Teaching explicit encoding strategies such as organising material, creating mental associations, and using systematic methods, since older adults can improve their encoding skills through instruction
option_d: Focusing only on recognition tasks and avoiding all recall exercises
rationale: The course material states that older adults can improve their encoding skills through training or instruction. It also notes that older people are less likely to spontaneously arrange material or create mental associations. Teaching explicit encoding strategies directly addresses this gap and demonstrates to educators and caregivers that older adults can actively improve their memory performance.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A doctor is explaining medication instructions to an 80-year-old patient. The patient is likely to remember the instructions better if the doctor:
option_a: Uses multiple formats — written instructions, visual aids, and organises the information clearly — recognising that older adults benefit from structured encoding support
option_b: Assumes the patient will not remember anything
option_c: Gives all instructions at once without repetition
option_d: Provides the information verbally only
rationale: The course material notes that older adults are less efficient at spontaneously arranging material or creating mental associations, but can improve through instruction and structured support. Providing written instructions (recognition-based support), visual aids, and clearly organised information compensates for encoding and retrieval challenges while respecting the patient's capacity to learn.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material identifies encoding, storage, and retrieval as three processes where memory declines in older adults. Which analysis best explains why retrieval problems are distinct from storage problems?
option_a: Storage problems involve deterioration of stored material over time, while retrieval problems involve difficulty accessing information that may still be stored — older adults can often recognise what they cannot recall
option_b: Retrieval and storage problems are identical
option_c: Storage problems only affect short-term memory
option_d: Retrieval problems mean the information was never stored
rationale: The course material distinguishes between storage problems (stored material may deteriorate to the point where retrieval becomes difficult or impossible) and retrieval problems (difficulty in accessing learned information from memory). The key evidence for this distinction is that older adults can often recognise items they know (suggesting the information is still stored) even when they cannot recall them on open-ended tasks (retrieval difficulty).
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher finds that older adults who attended a memory training programme showed significant improvement in recall tasks. Which inference from the course material best explains why this training was effective?
option_a: Training made their storage capacity equal to that of young adults
option_b: Training eliminated all retrieval problems permanently
option_c: Training reversed the aging process in their brains
option_d: Training addressed encoding deficits by teaching explicit strategies for organising and associating material, which older adults do not spontaneously use but can learn
rationale: The course material states that older adults are less likely to spontaneously arrange material or create mental associations (encoding problems) but can improve their encoding skills through training or instruction. Memory training programmes are effective because they teach explicit encoding strategies that compensate for the reduced spontaneous use of such strategies, not because they reverse biological aging.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material states that younger adults can remember word pairs or paragraphs better than older people after several hours, days, or weeks. Which combination of memory processes best explains this difference?
option_a: Older adults lack the ability to form any new memories
option_b: Only retrieval problems
option_c: A combination of less efficient initial encoding, possible storage deterioration over time, and retrieval difficulties
option_d: Only storage problems
rationale: The course material identifies declines in all three memory processes in older adults: encoding problems (decreased efficiency in arranging material), storage problems (deterioration of stored material over time), and retrieval problems (difficulty accessing learned information). The finding that younger adults remember better after extended periods likely reflects a combination of all three processes — less efficient initial encoding, gradual storage deterioration, and greater retrieval difficulty.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A colleague tells you, "There's no point teaching new skills to older adults because their memory is too poor to retain anything new." Which is the strongest evidence-based evaluation of this statement?
option_a: Memory training is never effective for older adults
option_b: The statement is completely true
option_c: The statement is false and harmful — the course material shows older adults can improve encoding through instruction, and recognition performance is nearly equal to younger adults
option_d: Older adults can only learn simple skills
rationale: The course material provides multiple pieces of evidence contradicting this statement: older adults can improve their encoding skills through training or instruction; they perform almost as well as younger adults on recognition tasks; and their memory difficulties are primarily with spontaneous encoding strategies and open-ended recall, not with all forms of learning. The statement is not only incorrect but potentially harmful if it discourages educational opportunities for older adults.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A public health educator is designing materials about health screenings for older adults. She wants to maximise retention of important information. Based on the course material, which design choice is most likely to be effective?
option_a: Information delivered only by phone
option_b: Long, detailed paragraphs with no visual organisation
option_c: Organised, bulleted information with categories, visual cues, and repeated exposure to support encoding and recognition
option_d: A single brief statement with no details
rationale: Given that older adults are less likely to spontaneously arrange material but can improve encoding through instruction and structure, organised materials with visual cues and categories directly support encoding. The strong performance on recognition tasks suggests that structured, visually organised materials with repeated exposure would maximise information retention. Long unstructured paragraphs would challenge spontaneous encoding, while overly brief statements might lack sufficient detail.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: What is the primary psychosocial task of late adulthood according to Erikson's final stage?
option_a: Establishing identity
option_b: Achieving intimacy in relationships
option_c: Maintaining ego integrity while avoiding despair
option_d: Developing generativity
rationale: Erikson concluded that the primary psychosocial task of late adulthood (65 and beyond) is to maintain ego integrity (holding on to one's sense of wholeness) while avoiding despair (fearing there is too little time to begin a new life course). Those who succeed at this final task also develop wisdom, which includes accepting without major regrets the life that one has lived, as well as the inescapability of death.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to the course material, what does wisdom in Erikson's final stage include?
option_a: Accepting without major regrets the life one has lived and the inescapability of death
option_b: Achieving financial security
option_c: Maintaining physical health
option_d: Accumulating the most knowledge possible
rationale: Wisdom, the positive outcome of Erikson's final stage, includes accepting without major regrets the life that one has lived, as well as the inescapability of death. Those who achieve ego integrity can accept inevitable disease and impending death without fear of succumbing helplessly. However, even older adults with high integrity may feel some despair when wondering if another path may have been happier.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: An 80-year-old reflects on her life, acknowledging both joys and sorrows, feeling fundamentally content with the choices she made, while occasionally wondering what might have been if she had taken a different career path. Which concept best describes her state?
option_a: Predominantly ego integrity with brief moments of despair, which is normal even for those with high integrity
option_b: Complete despair over missed opportunities
option_c: Avoidance of all reflection on the past
option_d: Pure ego integrity without any despair
rationale: The course material explicitly states that even older adults who achieve a high degree of integrity may feel some despair at this stage as they contemplate their past. No one makes it through life without wondering if another path may have been happier and more productive. This woman's fundamental contentment with occasional wondering represents predominantly ego integrity with normal brief moments of despair.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why does despair in Erikson's final stage lead to fear of death?
option_a: Because physical pain causes fear of death
option_b: Because of religious beliefs only
option_c: Because all older adults fear death equally
option_d: Because looking back on life as a series of missed opportunities fills the individual with bitterness, and death symbolises emptiness and failure
rationale: The course material states that if a person looks back on life as a series of missed opportunities or filled with personal misfortunes, the sense is of bitter despair. Then death is viewed with fear because it symbolises emptiness and failure. In contrast, integrity of the self allows an individual to accept inevitable disease and death without fear of succumbing helplessly.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A hospice counsellor is working with a terminally ill 78-year-old man who expresses peace with his life, speaks fondly of his family and career, and says "I've had a good run." However, he occasionally expresses regret about not reconciling with his estranged brother. Which counselling approach best reflects the course material's understanding of this stage?
option_a: Suggest he contact his brother regardless of circumstances
option_b: Tell him he should not feel any regret
option_c: Help him find peace with his predominant integrity while acknowledging that brief moments of regret are normal even for those with high integrity
option_d: Focus only on his regret about his brother
rationale: The course material states that even older adults who achieve a high degree of integrity may feel some despair when wondering if another path may have been happier. No one makes it through life without some regrets. The counsellor should validate the man's predominant sense of peace and integrity while acknowledging that his brief regret is a normal part of this stage, potentially helping him find resolution if possible.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A gerontological social worker meets two older adults in a care facility. One frequently shares life stories, mentors younger residents, and seems at peace despite health challenges. The other is bitter, constantly mentions "what might have been," and fears death intensely. Which Eriksonian analysis best applies?
option_a: The first demonstrates ego integrity and wisdom; the second demonstrates despair characterised by bitterness and fear of death as a symbol of emptiness
option_b: Both are experiencing normal aging with no psychological differences
option_c: The second individual is medically ill, not psychologically distressed
option_d: Both individuals have achieved ego integrity
rationale: The first individual's sharing of life stories, mentoring, and peace despite challenges demonstrates ego integrity and wisdom. The second individual's bitterness, preoccupation with missed opportunities, and intense fear of death exemplify despair — the negative outcome of Erikson's final stage where death symbolises emptiness and failure rather than a natural conclusion to a meaningful life.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A life review therapy programme for older adults encourages participants to reflect on their lives, share stories, and find meaning in both positive and negative experiences. Which theoretical basis from the course material best supports this approach?
option_a: The programme helps older adults achieve ego integrity by accepting their life as lived without major regrets, which brings peace and reduces fear of death
option_b: The programme helps older adults forget their past
option_c: The programme is purely for entertainment
option_d: The programme prevents physical aging
rationale: Erikson's final stage involves maintaining ego integrity — holding on to one's sense of wholeness and accepting without major regrets the life one has lived. Life review therapy directly addresses this by helping older adults reflect on and find meaning in their life experiences. Achieving integrity brings peace and allows acceptance of death, while failure leads to despair and fear of death as emptiness.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A colleague claims that "achieving ego integrity means an older adult will never feel sadness or regret about their life." Which is the strongest evaluation of this claim?
option_a: Only perfectly lived lives produce ego integrity
option_b: The claim is fully accurate
option_c: The claim is false — the course material explicitly states that even those with high integrity may experience brief moments of despair when wondering if another path would have been happier
option_d: Ego integrity is impossible to achieve
rationale: The course material explicitly states: "However, even older adults who achieve a high degree of integrity may feel some despair at this stage as they contemplate their past. No one makes it through life without wondering if another path may have been happier and more productive." The claim that integrity eliminates all sadness or regret directly contradicts this statement.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A nursing home implements a policy of keeping older adults busy with constant activities to prevent them from reflecting on their lives, arguing that such reflection causes depression. Which is the strongest evaluation of this policy?
option_a: Older adults should never reflect on their past
option_b: Constant busyness is the only path to happiness
option_c: This is an excellent approach that prevents all depression
option_d: The policy is counterproductive — reflection and life review are essential for achieving ego integrity, and preventing this process may actually increase despair
rationale: Erikson's framework identifies the primary task of late adulthood as maintaining ego integrity through reflection on one's life. Preventing older adults from engaging in this reflection denies them the opportunity to achieve integrity and wisdom. The policy may actually increase despair by suppressing the natural developmental process of coming to terms with one's life, leading to greater fear of death and bitterness.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to disengagement theory, how does aging affect the relationship between older adults and society?
option_a: Older adults should maintain the same level of activity as in middle age
option_b: Society forces older adults into complete isolation
option_c: Older adults should increase their social involvement
option_d: Aging is viewed as a mutual withdrawal where older adults voluntarily slow down and step back from society, benefiting both individuals and society
rationale: Disengagement theory views aging as a process of mutual withdrawal in which older adults voluntarily slow down by retiring, as expected by society. Proponents hold that mutual social withdrawal benefits both individuals and society. This is one of three theories of socio-emotional development discussed in the course material, contrasted with activity theory and socio-emotional selectivity theory.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Which theory asserts a positive correlation between keeping socially and productively active and aging well?
option_a: Activity theory
option_b: Socio-emotional selectivity theory
option_c: Attachment theory
option_d: Disengagement theory
rationale: Activity theory sees a positive correlation between keeping active and aging well. The more active older adults are, the more likely they are to be satisfied with their lives. Greater activity, especially social and productive activity, was related to happiness, better functioning, and a lower mortality rate. This contrasts with disengagement theory's view of mutual withdrawal.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: An older adult has reduced her social circle to a small group of close family members and long-time friends, with whom she spends most of her time. She reports high emotional satisfaction and says she no longer has patience for superficial socialising. Which theory best explains her behaviour?
option_a: Disengagement theory
option_b: Activity theory
option_c: Socio-emotional selectivity theory — older adults become more selective about social networks, prioritising emotional satisfaction and familiar relationships
option_d: Social learning theory
rationale: Socio-emotional selectivity theory states that older adults become more selective about their social networks because they place a high value on emotional satisfaction. They often spend more time with familiar individuals with whom they have had rewarding relationships. This theory challenges the misconception that older adults are in emotional despair due to isolation; rather, they deliberately choose smaller networks to pursue emotion-related goals.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A community centre for older adults notices declining attendance at large social events but increasing participation in small, intimate discussion groups and family-style dinners. Which theoretical approach best explains this trend and should guide future programming?
option_a: Activity theory — the centre should only offer large events
option_b: The trend is random and meaningless
option_c: Disengagement theory — the centre should close all programmes
option_d: Socio-emotional selectivity theory — older adults prefer smaller, emotionally meaningful social interactions over large, superficial gatherings
rationale: The shift from large social events to small, intimate groups aligns with socio-emotional selectivity theory, which states that older adults become more selective about social networks, prioritising emotional satisfaction and familiar relationships. Programming should reflect this preference by offering more small-group, emotionally meaningful activities rather than interpreting declining large-event attendance as social withdrawal or disengagement.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A 75-year-old man retires and decides to volunteer three days a week, join a walking group, take a class at the local college, and maintain regular lunches with colleagues. He reports feeling happier and more energised than during his final years of work. Which theory best explains his experience?
option_a: Social isolation theory
option_b: Disengagement theory
option_c: Activity theory — greater social and productive activity is related to happiness and better functioning
option_d: Socio-emotional selectivity theory only
rationale: Activity theory asserts a positive correlation between keeping socially and productively active and aging well. The more active older adults are, the more likely they are to be satisfied with their lives. This man's increased activity through volunteering, exercise, education, and social lunches, combined with his reported happiness, directly exemplifies the activity theory prediction.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A family is concerned that their 82-year-old mother has stopped attending her large church congregation and now only meets with a small prayer group of long-time friends. She seems happier and less stressed than before. Which explanation best addresses their concerns?
option_a: She has developed dementia
option_b: She should be forced to attend the large congregation
option_c: She is depressed and isolating herself
option_d: Her behaviour aligns with socio-emotional selectivity theory — she is deliberately choosing a smaller, more emotionally meaningful social network, which is a normal and positive pattern in late adulthood
rationale: Socio-emotional selectivity theory states that older adults become more selective about social networks, prioritising emotional satisfaction and familiar relationships. The mother's shift from a large congregation to a small prayer group of long-time friends, combined with her increased happiness, demonstrates this deliberate selectivity. The theory challenges the misconception that reduced social network size indicates emotional despair.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The three theories of socio-emotional development offer different prescriptions for "successful aging." Which analysis best captures the core difference between disengagement theory and activity theory?
option_a: Disengagement theory views withdrawal as beneficial, while activity theory views continued activity as beneficial — they offer fundamentally opposing prescriptions for successful aging
option_b: Both theories are identical
option_c: Neither theory has any relevance to aging
option_d: Both theories agree that older adults should maintain maximum activity
rationale: Disengagement theory views aging as a mutual withdrawal where older adults voluntarily step back from society, asserting this benefits both individuals and society. Activity theory, in contrast, asserts a positive correlation between keeping active and aging well, with greater activity related to happiness, better functioning, and lower mortality. These are fundamentally opposing views on whether social withdrawal or continued activity promotes successful aging.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Socio-emotional selectivity theory challenges both disengagement theory and activity theory. Which analysis best captures this challenge?
option_a: It reframes reduced social activity not as withdrawal or failure to stay active, but as deliberate selectivity to prioritise emotionally meaningful relationships
option_b: It agrees completely with disengagement theory
option_c: It states that older adults should maintain the same social network size as young adults
option_d: It argues that older adults are in despair due to isolation
rationale: Socio-emotional selectivity theory challenges both previous theories by explaining that older adults' reduced social networks result from deliberate choice to pursue emotional satisfaction, not from societal pressure to withdraw (disengagement) or from failure to maintain activity (activity theory). It states that older adults choose to spend time with close families and friends, challenging the misconception that they are in emotional despair due to isolation.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A senior living facility requires all residents to participate in at least five group activities per week, arguing that "activity is the key to successful aging." Which is the strongest evaluation of this policy?
