option_a: Disengagement theory supports this policy
option_b: The policy is perfectly aligned with all theories of aging
option_c: The policy is overly rigid — while activity theory supports engagement, socio-emotional selectivity theory suggests that quality and emotional meaning matter more than quantity, and forced participation may reduce well-being
option_d: Forced activity is always beneficial regardless of preference
rationale: While activity theory supports the value of engagement, socio-emotional selectivity theory emphasises that older adults prioritise emotional satisfaction and may prefer fewer, more meaningful activities. Forced participation ignores individual preferences and the quality of social interactions. A more balanced approach would offer varied activity options while respecting residents' choices about which activities provide genuine emotional value.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A family member claims that their elderly relative is "giving up on life" because she has reduced her social circle and stopped attending large community events. Which evaluation best applies the course material's theories?
option_a: The family member may be misinterpreting socio-emotional selectivity — the relative may be deliberately choosing emotionally satisfying relationships rather than withdrawing due to despair
option_b: The elderly relative is definitely depressed
option_c: Large community events are the only measure of social health
option_d: The family member is correct — any reduction in social activity indicates despair
rationale: Socio-emotional selectivity theory explicitly challenges the misconception that older adults are in emotional despair because of reduced social networks. It states that older adults deliberately choose smaller networks to pursue emotion-related goals, spending more time with familiar individuals with whom they have had rewarding relationships. The family member's interpretation may reflect a misunderstanding of this normal developmental pattern.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A researcher concludes that disengagement theory is "completely wrong" because many older adults who stay active report high life satisfaction. Which is the strongest evaluation of this conclusion?
option_a: The conclusion is too absolute — while activity theory challenges disengagement theory, some older adults may voluntarily choose reduced activity for personal reasons, suggesting disengagement may apply to some individuals even if not universal
option_b: Disengagement theory applies to all older adults
option_c: Activity and disengagement theories are identical
option_d: The conclusion is fully supported — disengagement theory has no validity
rationale: The course material presents disengagement theory as one of three theories, noting that it views aging as mutual withdrawal that benefits both individuals and society. While activity theory provides strong evidence against the universality of disengagement, and socio-emotional selectivity theory offers a more nuanced explanation, concluding that disengagement is "completely wrong" ignores that some older adults may voluntarily choose reduced activity. The subsequent theories refined rather than entirely invalidated the observation that social networks often shrink in late adulthood.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: What is thanatology as defined in the course material?
option_a: A medical treatment for terminally ill patients
option_b: The fear of death
option_c: The formal study of death and dying, examining biological, psychological, and social responses to mortality
option_d: A religious practice for the deceased
rationale: Thanatology is the study of death and dying. Thanatologists examine all aspects of death, including biological (the cessation of physiological processes), psychological (cognitive, emotional, and behavioural responses), and social (historical, cultural, and legal issues). North American society has witnessed an increased interest in thanatology in recent years.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to the course material, what is the key distinction between "death" and "dying"?
option_a: Death is temporary, while dying is permanent
option_b: They are synonymous terms with identical meanings
option_c: Death is the absolute cessation of vital functions, while dying is the developmental process of losing these functions over time
option_d: Death refers only to physical processes, while dying refers only to emotional processes
rationale: Thanatologists carefully distinguish between "death" and "dying." Death may be considered the absolute cessation of vital functions, while dying is the process of losing these functions. Dying may also be seen as a developmental concomitant of living, a part of the birth-to-death continuum. These terms are not synonymous and have no unequivocal definitions.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why has North American society witnessed an increased interest in thanatology in recent years?
option_a: Because of growing recognition of the need to understand and improve end-of-life care, communication, and the psychological and social dimensions of death and dying
option_b: Because death is a new phenomenon
option_c: Because thanatology can prevent death
option_d: Because death rates have dramatically increased
rationale: The course material notes increased interest in thanatology without attributing it to increased death rates. The field examines biological, psychological, and social aspects of death, and research has addressed the critical role of open family communication in facilitating positive processing of death loss. The increased interest reflects growing recognition of the complexity of death and dying and the need for improved care and communication.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A hospital is developing an end-of-life care training programme for medical staff. Based on the course material's description of thanatology, which components should be included for a comprehensive approach?
option_a: Biological aspects (cessation of vital functions), psychological aspects (emotional and behavioural responses of patients and families), and social aspects (cultural, legal, and communication issues)
option_b: Only religious rituals for different faiths
option_c: Only legal paperwork requirements
option_d: Only biological aspects — how to certify death
rationale: Thanatology examines all aspects of death: biological (cessation of physiological processes), psychological (cognitive, emotional, and behavioural responses), and social (historical, cultural, and legal issues). A comprehensive end-of-life care training programme must address all three dimensions rather than focusing exclusively on biological, legal, or religious aspects.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A physician communicating with a terminally ill patient focuses exclusively on the biological processes of the disease and avoids discussing the patient's fears, feelings, or wishes for their remaining time. Which critique from a thanatological perspective best applies?
option_a: Physicians should never talk to terminally ill patients
option_b: Emotional discussions are harmful to terminally ill patients
option_c: The physician is correctly focusing on the most important aspect
option_d: The approach is incomplete — thanatology recognises that death involves psychological and social dimensions as well as biological ones, and the patient may have important emotional and practical concerns
rationale: Thanatology examines biological, psychological, and social aspects of death and dying. A physician who focuses exclusively on biological processes while ignoring the patient's cognitive, emotional, and behavioural responses fails to address the full experience of dying. The course material emphasises that open communication about feelings, life quality, and relationships is of utmost importance for dying individuals.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material states that death can be defined biologically as "the permanent cessation of all vital functions" but also notes that "from broader psychological, societal and cultural perspectives, death is much more than the end stage of a biological process." Which analysis best captures this distinction?
option_a: Cultural definitions of death are always wrong
option_b: Death has no meaning beyond biology
option_c: The biological definition is the only valid definition
option_d: Death has both objective biological dimensions and subjective meaning dimensions that vary across individuals, cultures, and contexts
rationale: The course material presents death as having both an objective biological dimension (permanent cessation of vital functions including heartbeat, brain activity, and breathing) and subjective meaning dimensions that are "highly coloured by the attitudes of the individuals who hold them as well as the situational context." This acknowledges that while death has biological markers, its meaning is shaped by psychological, social, and cultural factors.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A medical school curriculum includes only one lecture on death certification in its entire programme, arguing that "death is a failure of medicine, not a subject for study." Which is the strongest evaluation of this approach based on thanatological principles?
option_a: Medical students do not need to learn about death
option_b: Death certification is the only skill physicians need
option_c: This is appropriate because medicine should only focus on curing disease
option_d: This approach is inadequate — thanatology recognises death and dying as important phenomena requiring multidisciplinary understanding, and physicians play a critical role in end-of-life care and communication
rationale: Thanatology is the formal study of death and dying examining biological, psychological, and social dimensions. Physicians have primary control of information that drives medical decision making and have responsibility for initiating discussions about advance directives. The course material notes that both patients and physicians agree that physicians have responsibility for these conversations. Reducing death to a single lecture on certification ignores the critical role physicians play in the psychological and social dimensions of dying.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Some critics argue that the study of thanatology is morbid and that focusing on death and dying creates unnecessary anxiety. Which is the strongest evaluation of this criticism based on the course material?
option_a: The criticism is contradicted by the material's finding that open communication about death facilitates healthy adjustment, and that denial of death leads to existential anxiety
option_b: Thanatology has produced no useful knowledge
option_c: Death education is harmful to children
option_d: The criticism is valid — thanatology should not be studied
rationale: The course material presents evidence that open communication about death facilitates positive processing of death loss and healthy adjustment. Conversely, the denial of death leads to existential anxiety. Far from creating unnecessary anxiety, thanatology provides frameworks for understanding and communicating about death that reduce anxiety and improve end-of-life experiences for dying individuals and their families.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: According to existential psychologists like Rollo May, why must individuals accept the inevitability of death?
option_a: Because death should be feared
option_b: Because acceptance prevents death
option_c: Because denial is impossible
option_d: Because without accepting death, they cannot fully embrace or find true meaning in life
rationale: Existential psychologists like Rollo May believe that individuals must accept the inevitability of their own deaths and the deaths of loved ones; otherwise, they cannot fully embrace or find true meaning in life. The denial of death leads to existential anxiety, which can be a source of emotional troubles in daily life. Conversely, the more purpose and meaning individuals see in their lives, the less they fear death.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: How does existential anxiety arise according to the course material?
option_a: From accepting death too early
option_b: From religious beliefs about the afterlife
option_c: From thinking about death too much
option_d: From the denial of death and the failure to find true meaning in life
rationale: Existential anxiety is described as a source of emotional trouble in daily life that stems from the denial of death and the failure to find true meaning in life. The course material contrasts this with the belief that thinking about death is harmful; existentialists argue that denying death leads to anxiety, while accepting it allows individuals to fully embrace life.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A middle-aged man who has avoided thinking about death his entire life suddenly develops severe anxiety, restlessness, and feelings of meaninglessness after the death of a close friend. An existential therapist would most likely interpret this as:
option_a: Existential anxiety triggered by the confrontation with mortality that had been previously denied, requiring acceptance of death to find meaning in life
option_b: A sign of clinical depression requiring medication only
option_c: Evidence that he should avoid all future funerals
option_d: A normal grief reaction with no deeper significance
rationale: The course material states that existential anxiety stems from the denial of death and failure to find true meaning in life. The friend's death forced a confrontation with mortality that the man had spent his life avoiding. Existential psychology would interpret his symptoms as the emotional consequence of this denied mortality breaking through, and would work toward helping him accept death's inevitability as a path to finding meaning.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A therapist working with older adults notices that clients who report having strong purpose and meaning in their lives show significantly less fear of death than those who feel their lives lack purpose. Which concept from the course material best explains this finding?
option_a: Existential psychology's principle that meaning in life reduces death fear, and denial of death creates existential anxiety
option_b: Disengagement theory
option_c: Activity theory
option_d: Kubler-Ross's stages of dying
rationale: The course material explicitly states that research indicates the more purpose and meaning that individuals see in their lives, the less they fear death. This is a foundational principle of existential psychology — finding meaning in life is directly tied to reducing the fear of dying. Conversely, the denial of death and failure to find meaning leads to existential anxiety.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A wellness guru claims that "the secret to happiness is to never think about death and to focus only on positive thoughts." Which is the strongest evaluation of this advice based on existential psychology principles from the course material?
option_a: Thinking about death is always harmful
option_b: Positive thinking is the only path to mental health
option_c: The advice is fully supported by existential psychology
option_d: The advice contradicts existential psychology, which argues that denying death leads to existential anxiety while accepting mortality allows individuals to fully embrace and find meaning in life
rationale: Existential psychologists like Rollo May believe that individuals must accept the inevitability of their own deaths; otherwise, they cannot fully embrace or find true meaning in life. The denial of death leads to existential anxiety. The guru's advice to "never think about death" directly contradicts this principle and may actually increase rather than decrease anxiety by promoting denial rather than acceptance.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Some health professionals avoid discussing death with terminally ill patients, believing that "talking about death will make them give up hope." Which is the strongest evaluation of this belief based on the course material?
option_a: Healthcare professionals should always make decisions for patients
option_b: The belief is fully supported by research
option_c: The belief is contradicted by evidence that open communication about death facilitates healthy adjustment, while avoidance creates additional suffering and denies patients the opportunity to find meaning in their remaining time
option_d: Patients never want to discuss death
rationale: The course material presents extensive evidence that open communication about death, dying, and bereavement facilitates positive processing of death loss and healthy adjustment. It notes that avoiding the topic makes dying a "forbidden topic" and that relationships allowing for communication about death precede healthy adjustment. Avoiding the discussion denies patients the opportunity to find meaning, resolve relationships, and prepare for death in ways consistent with their values.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material contrasts existential psychology's view with the common belief that thinking about death is harmful. Which analysis best captures why existential psychologists argue that accepting death is essential for living fully?
option_a: They believe acceptance of death shortens life
option_b: They believe death is pleasant
option_c: They argue that awareness of mortality creates urgency to find meaning and live authentically, while denial produces inauthentic living and anxiety
option_d: They want to increase people's fear of death
rationale: Existential psychology argues that awareness of mortality is a catalyst for authentic living. The denial of death leads to existential anxiety and emotional troubles in daily life, while acceptance allows individuals to embrace life fully and find true meaning. Research supports this: the more purpose and meaning individuals see in their lives, the less they fear death. This creates a positive cycle where acceptance of mortality leads to more meaningful living.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: What is endocannibalism as described in the course material?
option_a: A type of cremation ritual
option_b: A Buddhist funeral ceremony
option_c: The practice of preserving bodies after death
option_d: The practice of eating the dead to forge a permanent connection with them and cathartically express fear associated with death
rationale: Endocannibalism refers to the practice of eating the dead to forge a permanent connection between the living and the recently deceased. It is also described as a cathartic way to express the loathing and fear associated with death and its tragic aftermath. Cultures who engaged in endocannibalism included the Melanesians of Papua New Guinea and the Wari people of Brazil.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: In Tibetan Buddhist sky burials, what happens to the body of the deceased?
option_a: It is buried in the ground facing Mecca
option_b: It is cremated and ashes scattered in a river
option_c: It is preserved in a tomb
option_d: It is chopped into small pieces and given to birds (particularly vultures) as a final gesture of compassion and charity
rationale: Tibetan Buddhist "Sky Burials" involve chopping up the dead into small pieces and giving the remains to animals, particularly birds. While this may seem undignified to outsiders, from a Buddhist perspective, the dead body is seen as an empty vessel, and the ritual is viewed as a final gesture of compassion and charity — one's remains used to sustain the life of another living creature. Today, over 80% of Tibetan Buddhists choose sky burial.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: An anthropology student is disturbed after learning about Tibetan sky burials, describing the practice as "disgusting and disrespectful to the dead." Which response from the course material would best help her understand the practice?
option_a: Suggest she avoid learning about other cultures
option_b: Tell her that her reaction is wrong
option_c: Explain that from the Buddhist perspective, the dead body is an empty vessel, and the ritual makes complete sense as a gesture of compassion and charity — our perceptions of rituals are coloured by our own cultural and religious frameworks
option_d: Agree that the practice is objectively disrespectful
rationale: The course material states that while sky burials may seem undignified or disgusting to outsiders, they make complete sense from a Buddhist perspective. Buddhists have no desire to preserve or commemorate a dead body (seen as an empty vessel), and in tune with their respect for all life, Buddhists see it as fitting that one's final act sustains another living creature. The material emphasises that our perceptions of death rituals are coloured by our own attitudes and contexts.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A psychology educator wants to teach students about how different cultures process the emotional tragedy of mortality. Which example from the course material best demonstrates how a death ritual can serve psychological functions of connection and emotional expression?
option_a: A standard insurance policy
option_b: A legal will
option_c: A modern embalming procedure
option_d: Endocannibalism — described as a way to forge a permanent connection with the deceased and cathartically express fear associated with death
rationale: Endocannibalism is explicitly described as a way to forge a permanent connection between the living and the recently deceased, and as a cathartic way to express the loathing and fear associated with death. The material notes that some anthropologists have suggested it was something the dead would have expected — a final gesture of goodwill to the tribe and family. This directly links the ritual to psychological functions of connection and emotional processing.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material presents three very different cultural death rituals: endocannibalism, sky burials, and Sati. Which common psychological thread connects all three?
option_a: All three are universally viewed positively
option_b: All three have identical religious origins
option_c: All three practices are still widely practised today
option_d: All three reflect attempts to process death's emotional impact, maintain social bonds, and find meaning in mortality within specific cultural and religious frameworks
rationale: Despite their dramatically different forms, all three rituals serve psychological and social functions: endocannibalism forges connection and cathartically expresses fear; sky burials represent compassion and charity within Buddhist beliefs about the body as an empty vessel; and Sati (though now banned and viewed negatively) was historically understood within specific cultural frameworks about afterlife togetherness. The common thread is that cultures develop rituals to process death's emotional impact and find meaning within their belief systems.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Why does the course material note that sky burials "may seem undignified and even a bit disgusting" to outsiders while being meaningful to Tibetan Buddhists?
option_a: To prove that Buddhist practices are superior
option_b: To criticise the practice
option_c: To illustrate how perceptions of death rituals are culturally relative — what seems disrespectful in one cultural framework can be deeply respectful in another
option_d: To suggest outsiders should adopt the practice
rationale: The course material states that the meanings of death are highly coloured by the attitudes of individuals and their situational context. The contrast between outsider perceptions ("undignified") and insider meaning ("compassion and charity") illustrates cultural relativity — our judgments about death practices are shaped by our own cultural frameworks rather than by objective standards of dignity. Buddhists view the body as an empty vessel and the ritual as a respectful final act of giving.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A museum exhibit on world cultures includes a display on Sati, the banned Indian practice where widows immolated themselves on their husband's funeral pyre. A visitor complains that the display "glorifies a barbaric practice." Which is the strongest evaluation of the museum's approach based on the course material?
option_a: The museum's inclusion is educationally appropriate if presented in historical context, as the course material uses these examples to help students understand how diverse societies process mortality — the goal is understanding, not glorification
option_b: The museum should only display Western funeral practices
option_c: Sati should be presented as a model for modern practice
option_d: The museum should remove the display immediately
rationale: The course material presents cultural death rituals specifically to help psychology students understand how diverse societies process the emotional tragedy of mortality and conceptualise the afterlife. The material notes that Sati is "mercifully banned" and presents it in historical context. Educational presentation of such practices aims at cross-cultural understanding of how different societies grapple with death, not glorification or endorsement.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A student concludes that "endocannibalism proves that some cultures are primitive and uncivilised compared to modern Western funeral practices." Which is the strongest evaluation of this conclusion?
option_a: The conclusion reflects cultural bias rather than objective analysis — the course material presents endocannibalism as having specific psychological and social functions (forging connections, expressing fear) within its cultural context, not as evidence of primitiveness
option_b: Endocannibalism has no cultural meaning
option_c: Western funeral practices are universally superior
option_d: The conclusion is objectively true
rationale: The course material presents endocannibalism as a cultural practice with specific psychological functions — forging permanent connections with the deceased and cathartically expressing fear associated with death. Anthropologists suggest it was expected by the dead as a final gesture of goodwill. Interpreting this practice as "primitive" imposes external cultural judgments rather than understanding the ritual's meaning within its own cultural framework, which the material emphasises is essential for understanding how diverse societies process mortality.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: How many stages of dying did Elisabeth Kubler-Ross identify through her systematic study of death and dying?
option_a: Three stages
option_b: Four stages
option_c: Five stages
option_d: Seven stages
rationale: Elisabeth Kubler-Ross, a Swiss-born psychiatrist who began a systematic study of death and dying at the University of Chicago, identified five stages in the dying process after interviewing more than 200 dying patients. The five stages are: (1) denial and isolation, (2) anger, (3) bargaining, (4) depression, and (5) acceptance.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Which of the following is NOT one of Kubler-Ross's five stages of dying?
option_a: Denial and isolation
option_b: Anger
option_c: Bargaining
option_d: Shock
rationale: Kubler-Ross identified five stages: (1) denial and isolation, (2) anger, (3) bargaining, (4) depression, and (5) acceptance. While denial may involve feelings of shock and disbelief, "shock" is not listed as a separate stage. The five stages are not necessarily sequential — not all people progress predictably through all stages, nor do they experience them in one particular order.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: In Kubler-Ross's framework, a terminally ill patient says, "I will quit smoking, drinking, and lose weight if I can live to see my granddaughter marry." Which stage does this statement best represent?
option_a: Denial
option_b: Anger
option_c: Bargaining — an attempt to delay or postpone death's inevitability by promising good behaviour
option_d: Acceptance
rationale: Bargaining is described as an attempt to delay or postpone death's inevitability. Terminally ill people may believe they can bargain for an extension of life because rewards often follow good behaviour. The example in the course material closely matches this statement: "I will quit smoking, drinking, and lose weight if I can live to see my granddaughter marry."
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: What is the key difference between reactive depression and preparatory depression in Kubler-Ross's framework?
option_a: Reactive depression focuses on past losses and may involve guilt or shame, while preparatory depression takes into account impending losses to come
option_b: Reactive depression is always more severe
option_c: Preparatory depression occurs before the diagnosis
option_d: There is no difference between them
rationale: Depression in Kubler-Ross's framework consists of two types: reactive depression focuses on past losses and may be accompanied by guilt or shame. In contrast, preparatory depression takes into account all the impending losses to come: loss of self, loss of relationship, and all that one has ever known and loved. Understanding this distinction helps caregivers provide appropriate support.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A terminally ill patient initially refuses to believe the diagnosis, saying "There must be some mistake." She avoids discussions about her condition and asks to see another doctor. Family members are frustrated by what they see as denial. Which response best aligns with Kubler-Ross's framework?
option_a: Ignore her feelings and proceed with treatment planning without her input
option_b: Force the patient to confront reality immediately
option_c: Recognise this as denial and isolation — a common initial response characterised by shock and disbelief that serves as a healthy defence, providing a brief respite before confronting reality
option_d: Tell the family she is being irrational
rationale: Denial and isolation is the most common response in the initial stage, characterised by feelings of shock, disbelief, and numbness. Kubler-Ross described denial as a healthy defence in the short run — as a buffer, it provides a brief respite, allowing people the opportunity to confront the reality of dying and to mobilise other less radical defences. Forcing immediate confrontation would be counterproductive.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A dying patient lashes out at his family, yelling at his wife for small mistakes and expressing rage that his colleague — who smoked and drank heavily — remains healthy while he is dying. The family is hurt and considering limiting visits. Which interpretation and response best aligns with Kubler-Ross's framework?
option_a: The family should immediately stop visiting
option_b: The patient needs to be sedated
option_c: The patient is becoming a bad person and deserves to be alone
option_d: This is the anger stage — a cry of anguish about the untimeliness of death that is typically displaced on loved ones; the family should be supported to understand this is not personal
rationale: Anger provides people with the chance to vent their rage and resentment about dying. Kubler-Ross noted it is not uncommon for anger to be displaced on loved ones and caregivers. The displacement is typically not personal but a cry of anguish at the untimeliness of death, enviousness of those with good health, or about all that will be left uncompleted and unfulfilled. Supporting the family to understand this dynamic helps them respond with compassion rather than withdrawal.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A hospice nurse is caring for a patient who, after several months of illness, has become quiet, withdrawn, and seems at peace with the situation. The patient says, "I'm ready." Which stage does this represent, and what is the most appropriate nursing response?
option_a: Depression — the nurse should try to cheer the patient up
option_b: Denial — the nurse should force reality confrontation
option_c: Acceptance — a detached state of peace where the patient has accepted death's inevitability; the nurse should provide quiet presence and comfort
option_d: Anger — the nurse should prepare for outbursts
rationale: Acceptance is described as a detached state of peace where the individual accepts death's inevitability. In this stage, people may be weak, withdrawn, and uncommunicative with others. Kubler-Ross quoted a patient: "It is as if the pain is gone, the struggle is over, and there comes a time for the 'final rest before the journey.'" The appropriate response is quiet presence and comfort rather than trying to "cheer up" the patient or force emotional expression.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A dying woman's daughter tells the medical team, "My mother has accepted her diagnosis, so I'm confused why she suddenly got angry at the nurse yesterday and insisted the doctors were wrong." Which explanation best applies Kubler-Ross's framework?
option_a: Kubler-Ross's stages are not necessarily sequential; patients may move back and forth between stages, and acceptance of one aspect of dying does not preclude anger or denial about another aspect
option_b: The mother has definitely recovered
option_c: The stages always occur in strict order
option_d: The mother is faking her illness
rationale: Kubler-Ross explicitly pointed out that although the five stages are typical, they are not absolute. Not all people progress predictably through all the stages, nor do people experience the stages in one particular order. Additionally, these stages do not necessarily represent the healthiest pattern for all individuals under all circumstances. Patients may move between stages as different aspects of their situation become salient.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A family whose father is dying asks the hospice team why he has not reached the "acceptance" stage yet and whether they should be concerned. Which response best reflects Kubler-Ross's actual views about her stages?
option_a: "We can force him into acceptance with medication"
option_b: "He must reach acceptance to die peacefully"
option_c: "Kubler-Ross noted that not all people progress predictably through all stages, nor in a particular order — these are typical reactions, not requirements for a 'good death'"
option_d: "He is failing at dying correctly"
rationale: Kubler-Ross pointed out that the five stages are typical but not absolute. Not all people progress predictably through all the stages, nor do people experience the stages in one particular order. Additionally, she noted that these stages do not necessarily represent the healthiest pattern for all individuals under all circumstances. The stages describe common reactions, not a prescribed pathway that everyone must follow.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material states that Kubler-Ross and others noted that "people whose loved ones are dying may progress through the same five stages as the dying person." Which implication of this finding is most significant for family-centred end-of-life care?
option_a: Families do not need any support
option_b: All family members experience the stages in the same order
option_c: Only the dying person experiences emotional responses
option_d: Family members may simultaneously be in different stages than the patient (e.g., the patient has accepted death while the family is still in denial), creating communication difficulties and requiring separate support
rationale: If both the dying person and their family members may progress through the same five stages, and these stages are not sequential, significant mismatches can occur. A patient who has reached acceptance may have family members still in denial or anger. These mismatched emotional states can create communication barriers and conflict, highlighting the need for family-centred support alongside patient care.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material notes that denial "serves as a healthy defence" in the short run. Which analysis best explains why denial can be psychologically beneficial rather than purely negative?
option_a: Denial provides a buffer that allows the individual time to gradually absorb the reality of dying and mobilise other, less radical defences at their own pace
option_b: Denial means the person will recover
option_c: Denial should always be maintained
option_d: Denial prevents all emotional processing
rationale: The course material states that in the short run, denial serves as a healthy defence for dealing with painful, difficult situations. As a buffer, denial provides a brief respite, allowing people the opportunity to confront the reality of dying and to mobilise other less radical defences. This "emotional shock absorber" function helps explain why denial is a common initial response — it protects the psyche from being overwhelmed by traumatic news all at once.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A common misconception is that Kubler-Ross's stages represent a predictable, sequential, or "healthiest" pattern. Which analysis from the course material most directly challenges this misconception?
option_a: All dying patients go through all five stages in order
option_b: The stages are medically required
option_c: Kubler-Ross stated that the stages are absolute and must be experienced in order
option_d: Kubler-Ross pointed out that the stages are not absolute, not necessarily sequential, and do not represent the healthiest pattern for all individuals under all circumstances
rationale: The course material explicitly states that Kubler-Ross pointed out the five stages are typical but not absolute, not all people progress predictably through all stages, nor do people experience them in one particular order. Additionally, she noted these stages do not necessarily represent the healthiest pattern for all individuals under all circumstances. This directly contradicts the common misconception that the stages are a prescribed sequential pathway.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A hospice brochure describes Kubler-Ross's five stages as "the five steps every dying person must complete to achieve a peaceful death." Which is the strongest evaluation of this description?
option_a: The description misrepresents Kubler-Ross's work by presenting the stages as sequential requirements rather than typical, non-absolute reactions that not all patients experience in order
option_b: The description should add more stages
option_c: Every dying person must complete all five stages
option_d: The description is fully accurate
rationale: The course material explicitly states that Kubler-Ross noted the five stages are typical but not absolute, not all people progress predictably through all stages, nor in a particular order, and they do not necessarily represent the healthiest pattern for all individuals. Presenting them as "steps every dying person must complete" fundamentally misrepresents this nuance and may create harmful expectations for patients and families.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A nurse tells a colleague, "Our patient has been angry for two weeks — she's stuck in the anger stage and not progressing properly." Which is the strongest evaluation of the nurse's interpretation?
option_a: Anger is not one of the stages
option_b: Two weeks is too long for any stage
option_c: The nurse is correctly applying Kubler-Ross's framework
option_d: The nurse is misunderstanding the framework — Kubler-Ross did not intend the stages to be seen as a linear progression with a set timeline, and "anger" may re-emerge throughout the dying process
rationale: Kubler-Ross explicitly stated that not all people progress predictably through the stages, nor do they experience them in one particular order. The stages describe typical emotional reactions, not a linear progression with set timelines. Anger may re-emerge at different points in the dying process, and there is no "proper" timeline for moving through or between stages. The nurse's interpretation imposes an inappropriate linear model on a non-linear framework.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: Some critics argue that Kubler-Ross's stages have been overgeneralised and should no longer be used in palliative care. Which is the most balanced evaluation of this critique based on the course material?
option_a: While the stages have been overgeneralised and misrepresented as sequential requirements, they remain a valuable framework for understanding common emotional reactions to dying when applied with appropriate flexibility
option_b: The stages have no value whatsoever
option_c: Palliative care should never reference emotional reactions
option_d: The stages are perfect and should be used exactly as written
rationale: The course material presents Kubler-Ross's stages as a well-known framework for understanding the emotional process of dying while also noting significant limitations: they are not absolute, not sequential, and not the healthiest pattern for all. The material uses the stages as a descriptive tool for empathising with terminally ill patients and providing support. A balanced evaluation recognises both their value as a framework for understanding common reactions and the need for flexible, individualised application.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: A hospital uses a checklist that requires staff to document which of Kubler-Ross's five stages a dying patient is in at each shift, with the goal of "moving patients through to acceptance." Which is the strongest evaluation of this practice?
option_a: This practice fundamentally misunderstands Kubler-Ross's framework by treating fluid, non-sequential emotional reactions as discrete categories to be progressed through on a schedule
option_b: Checklists are always beneficial
option_c: All patients should be in acceptance within two weeks
option_d: This is an excellent quality improvement initiative
rationale: Kubler-Ross explicitly stated that the stages are not absolute, not necessarily sequential, do not represent the healthiest pattern for all, and not all people experience all stages. Reducing these fluid emotional reactions to checklist categories and setting goals to "move patients through" them imposes a rigid, linear framework on what was intended as a descriptive, flexible model. This approach may pressure patients and staff to meet artificial milestones rather than respond to individual needs.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts

course_name: PSY 206 – Developmental Psychology II (Adulthood and Aging)
stem: The course material presents Kubler-Ross's work as "perhaps the best known investigation of the process of dying" while also noting significant limitations and misconceptions about her stages. Which conclusion best captures the course material's overall assessment?
option_a: Kubler-Ross's stages are absolute laws of dying
option_b: No other research on dying exists
option_c: Kubler-Ross's work is worthless and should be discarded
option_d: Kubler-Ross's stages provide a valuable but frequently misunderstood framework — they describe common emotional reactions that help caregivers empathise with patients, but should not be treated as a rigid, sequential, universal pathway
rationale: The course material describes Kubler-Ross as "perhaps the best known investigator of the process of dying" and presents her five stages as useful for helping caregivers empathise with terminally ill patients. Simultaneously, it notes that Kubler-Ross herself pointed out the stages are not absolute, not sequential, and not the healthiest pattern for all. The overall assessment is that the framework has significant value when properly understood and applied flexibly.
source_ref: "PSY 206- DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOLGY II ADULTHOOD AND AGING.docx", Excerpts
